Document Purpose
This document serves as the official software development specification for building the Drone Delivery Navigation System.
Unlike a normal AI prompt, this document provides complete architectural, technical, UI/UX, and development guidelines required to build the project exactly as envisioned.
Every future development decision must follow this document.
No design modifications should be made unless explicitly approved.

SECTION 15
AI Developer Instructions 
1. Introduction
1.1 Project Title
Drone Delivery Navigation System
1.2 Project Category
Full Stack Web Application
Academic Project
Data Structures and Algorithms
1.3 Purpose of the Project
The Drone Delivery Navigation System is a modern web application designed to simulate how autonomous drones can be managed for package delivery operations.
The project combines modern web technologies with practical implementations of Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA), allowing users to manage fleets, optimize delivery routes, monitor package deliveries, and analyze operational performance through a premium command-center interface.
The project is intended to demonstrate both frontend/backend engineering skills and meaningful use of core DSA concepts.
1.4 Target Users
The system is designed for:
Drone Fleet Operators 
Logistics Managers 
Warehouse Administrators 
Academic Evaluators (DSA Project Demonstration) 
Students learning Data Structures and Algorithms 
2. Problem Statement
Modern logistics systems require intelligent software capable of monitoring fleets, planning efficient routes, tracking deliveries, and analyzing operational performance.
Traditional student projects often demonstrate DSA algorithms in isolation without connecting them to real-world applications.
This project addresses that limitation by integrating DSA concepts into a realistic drone delivery management platform.
Instead of presenting algorithms independently, each algorithm powers a meaningful application feature, enabling users to understand how data structures and algorithms are applied in industry-scale systems.
3. Vision Statement
The goal of this project is not merely to create another CRUD application.
The vision is to build a premium Drone Command Center that feels like software used by a modern logistics company while simultaneously serving as a practical demonstration of DSA concepts.
The application should maintain a clean, modern, Apple-inspired interface and emphasize clarity, usability, and technical correctness.
4. Core Philosophy
The project follows four fundamental principles:
1. Simplicity
The interface should remain clean, uncluttered, and easy to understand.
Every screen should have a clear purpose.
2. Professional Design
The UI should resemble a premium SaaS product rather than a traditional academic project.
Design inspiration includes:
Apple 
DJI 
Linear 
Stripe 
Notion 
3. Real DSA Integration
Algorithms must not be added merely for documentation.
Each DSA concept must directly power a feature visible within the application.
4. Modular Architecture
Every feature should be developed independently while maintaining consistency across the entire application.
Modules should be reusable, scalable, and easy to maintain.
5. High-Level User Journey
The application follows the following flow:
Landing Page
      ↓
Login
      ↓
Dashboard
      ↓
Fleet Management
      ↓
Route Optimization
      ↓
Delivery Management
      ↓
Analytics Dashboard
Each module represents a distinct stage in the drone delivery workflow while sharing a consistent visual identity and navigation structure.
6. Project Highlights
The completed application will include:
Premium modern UI 
Responsive design 
JWT-based authentication 
Fleet management 
Route optimization using Mapbox GL JS 
Delivery lifecycle management 
Analytics dashboard 
MongoDB backend 
REST APIs 
Practical DSA implementations 
Modular React architecture 
7. Expected Learning Outcomes
Upon completion, the project should demonstrate proficiency in:
Frontend development with React and TypeScript 
Backend development using Node.js and Express 
Database modeling with MongoDB and Mongoose 
Authentication using JWT 
RESTful API development 
Map integration using Mapbox GL JS 
Application of Data Structures and Algorithms in real-world scenarios 
Software architecture and modular design 
Git-based version control 
Full-stack application development 
End of Section 1
Length:
 ~7 pages in a formatted document.
VOLUME 1
FOUNDATION & DEVELOPMENT GUIDELINES
SECTION 2
Project Vision, Objectives, Scope & Requirements
8. Project Vision
8.1 Vision Statement
The 
Drone Delivery Navigation System
 is envisioned as a modern, full-stack web application that simulates how autonomous drone fleets can be managed in a real-world logistics environment. Unlike conventional academic CRUD projects, this system combines elegant user experience, 
practical software engineering, and meaningful implementation of Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA).
The application should resemble a premium Drone Command Center used by organizations such as DJI, Amazon Prime Air, or Zipline, while remaining technically feasible for a university-level DSA project.
Every feature, screen, and workflow must contribute to a realistic simulation of drone delivery operations. The system should be visually appealing, easy to use, and architected using industry-standard practices.
8.2 Vision Goals
The project aims to achieve the following long-term goals:
Demonstrate practical applications of DSA in logistics and route planning. 
Showcase full-stack web development skills. 
Provide a premium, intuitive user interface inspired by modern SaaS products. 
Maintain a modular architecture for future scalability. 
Serve as both an academic submission and a professional portfolio project. 
8.3 Product Philosophy
The project follows a simple philosophy:
"Every algorithm should solve a real problem, and every interface should help the user understand the system naturally."
This means:
Algorithms should not exist only in the backend; they should visibly influence the application's behavior. 
The interface should communicate information clearly without overwhelming the user. 
Design decisions should prioritize usability and consistency over unnecessary complexity. 
9. Problem Statement
Existing Problem
Drone delivery is becoming an important area of logistics and transportation. Managing a fleet of autonomous drones requires solving several challenges:
Monitoring drone availability. 
Assigning the most suitable drone to deliveries. 
Calculating efficient delivery routes. 
Tracking delivery progress. 
Analyzing operational performance. 
Traditional educational projects often demonstrate algorithms independently without connecting them to practical software applications.
Proposed Solution
The Drone Delivery Navigation System integrates modern web development with DSA concepts to simulate a realistic drone logistics platform.
Each major system function is directly powered by one or more data structures or algorithms, allowing users to experience how theoretical concepts are applied in real-world software.
10. Project Objectives
Primary Objectives
Build a modern full-stack Drone Delivery Navigation System. 
Implement meaningful DSA algorithms throughout the application. 
Demonstrate frontend and backend integration. 
Provide an intuitive user experience. 
Ensure clean, maintainable, and scalable code. 
Academic Objectives
The project should demonstrate understanding of:
Arrays 
Searching Algorithms 
Sorting Algorithms 
Graph Data Structure 
Breadth-First Search (BFS) 
Dijkstra's Algorithm 
Queue 
Priority Queue 
Hash Maps 
CRUD Operations 
REST APIs 
Authentication 
MongoDB Integration 
Technical Objectives
The application should:
Use React with TypeScript for the frontend. 
Use Express.js for backend APIs. 
Store application data in MongoDB. 
Use Mapbox GL JS for route visualization. 
Protect private routes using JWT. 
Follow a modular component architecture. 
Support responsive layouts for desktop and tablet devices. 
11. Project Scope
Included Features
Authentication
User Login 
JWT Authentication 
Protected Dashboard 
Dashboard
Overview of the entire system 
Navigation to all modules 
System status 
Fleet Management
Drone inventory 
Drone status 
Search drones 
Sort drones 
Assign missions 
View drone details 
Route Optimization
Warehouse selection 
Destination selection 
Route calculation 
Interactive Mapbox map 
Route comparison 
No-fly zones 
Delivery Management
Delivery tracking 
Delivery timeline 
Package information 
Mission assignment 
Queue management 
Analytics Dashboard
KPI overview 
Delivery statistics 
Fleet performance 
Route analytics 
Heatmap 
Reports 
12. Out of Scope (Submission Version)
The following features are intentionally excluded from Version 1.0:
Three.js integration 
GSAP animations 
AI chatbot 
Voice assistant 
Multi-user collaboration 
Live IoT drone communication 
Real-time weather APIs 
Payment gateway 
Mobile application 
These may be added in future versions after project submission.
13. Functional Requirements
The system shall allow users to:
Authentication
Log in securely. 
Access protected routes. 
Log out safely. 
Fleet Management
View all drones. 
Search drones. 
Sort drones. 
Filter drones. 
Add new drones. 
Edit drone information. 
Delete drones. 
Route Optimization
Select warehouse. 
Select destination. 
Generate optimized routes. 
Display route on Mapbox. 
Show alternative routes. 
Highlight restricted areas. 
Delivery Management
View deliveries. 
Create deliveries. 
Assign drones. 
Track delivery progress. 
Manage delivery queue. 
Update delivery status. 
Analytics Dashboard
Display KPI metrics. 
Show delivery trends. 
Rank drones. 
Generate reports. 
Visualize delivery hotspots. 
14. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance
Pages should load quickly. 
Smooth user interactions. 
Efficient rendering of large drone lists. 
Usability
Modern interface. 
Consistent navigation. 
Easy-to-understand workflows. 
Scalability
Modular architecture. 
Reusable components. 
Easy to add future modules. 
Security
JWT Authentication. 
Password hashing using bcrypt. 
Protected backend routes. 
Input validation. 
Maintainability
Clean code. 
TypeScript interfaces. 
Component separation. 
Organized folder structure. 
15. Success Criteria
The project will be considered successful if it satisfies the following:
Functional Success
Authentication works correctly. 
All four modules function independently. 
CRUD operations work reliably. 
Mapbox integration displays routes correctly. 
DSA algorithms operate as intended. 
Technical Success
Clean project architecture. 
Proper API communication. 
Database operations perform correctly. 
Responsive design works across supported devices. 
No critical runtime errors. 
Academic Success
The project should clearly demonstrate practical implementation of:
Arrays 
Searching 
Sorting 
Queue 
Priority Queue 
Graph 
BFS 
Dijkstra 
Hash Maps 
Each concept should be directly linked to a visible application feature.
16. Project Constraints
The following constraints must always be respected during development:
Do not redesign the locked user interface. 
Keep the sidebar identical across all modules. 
Maintain the established color identity: 
Fleet → Blue 
Route → Green 
Delivery → Orange 
Analytics → Purple 
Use Mapbox GL JS for route visualization. 
Implement real DSA algorithms rather than placeholder logic. 
Follow the approved technology stack. 
Build reusable components. 
Prioritize readability and maintainability. 
End of Section 2

10–12 pages

SECTION 3
Technology Stack & Software Architecture
17. Technology Stack
17.1 Technology Selection Philosophy
The technology stack has been carefully selected based on four principles:
Alignment with the university syllabus. 
Suitability for a DSA-focused project. 
Industry relevance. 
Scalability for future enhancements. 
Every technology included must have a clear purpose. Unnecessary frameworks or libraries should not be introduced.
17.2 Frontend Technology Stack

<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="75927269" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00353B92" w:rsidRPr="00353B92" w:rsidRDefault="00353B92" w:rsidP="00353B92"><w:r w:rsidRPr="00353B92"><w:t>Backend API communication
React
The frontend must be built entirely using 
React with TypeScript
.
Reasons:
Component reusability 
Better maintainability 
Efficient rendering 
Scalable architecture 
Large ecosystem 
Industry standard 
React components should remain small and reusable.
Avoid creating extremely large components.
TypeScript
The entire project should use TypeScript.
Use interfaces for:
Drone 
User 
Route 
Delivery 
Analytics 
API responses 
Props 
Context objects 
Avoid using any unless absolutely necessary.
Tailwind CSS
Tailwind CSS will be used for all styling.
Reasons:
Faster development 
Utility-first approach 
Responsive design 
Clean component styling 
Do not mix Bootstrap or Material UI.
The design system must remain consistent with the approved glassmorphism UI.
React Router
Responsible for navigation.
Example routes:
/
 /
login
 /
dashboard
 /
fleet
 /
route
 /
delivery
 /
analytics
Each module should have its own dedicated page.
Axios
Axios should handle all communication between frontend and backend.
Avoid using the native Fetch API unless necessary.
Create a centralized Axios instance for:
Base URL 
Authentication headers 
Error handling 
18. Backend Technology Stack

<w:trPr><w:tblCellSpacing w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/></w:trPr><w:tc><w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="6858C18F" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00353B92" w:rsidRPr="00353B92" w:rsidRDefault="00353B92" w:rsidP="00353B92"><w:r w:rsidRPr="00353B92"><w:t>bcrypt
<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="30067DF2" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00353B92" w:rsidRPr="00353B92" w:rsidRDefault="00353B92" w:rsidP="00353B92"><w:r w:rsidRPr="00353B92"><w:t>Password hashing
<w:trPr><w:tblCellSpacing w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/></w:trPr><w:tc><w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="08D2EA3D" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00353B92" w:rsidRPr="00353B92" w:rsidRDefault="00353B92" w:rsidP="00353B92"><w:r w:rsidRPr="00353B92"><w:t>Mongoose
<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="1E70CE19" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00353B92" w:rsidRPr="00353B92" w:rsidRDefault="00353B92" w:rsidP="00353B92"><w:r w:rsidRPr="00353B92"><w:t>MongoDB ODM
Node.js
Node.js serves as the backend runtime.
Responsibilities:
Execute backend logic 
Handle requests 
Process DSA operations 
Communicate with MongoDB 
Express.js
Express is responsible for:
Routing 
Middleware 
Authentication 
CRUD APIs 
Error handling 
Project should follow REST architecture.
JWT Authentication
Authentication flow:
User Login
↓
Verify Credentials
↓
Generate JWT
↓
Return Token
↓
Store Token
↓
Protected Routes
Only authenticated users should access the Dashboard and modules.
bcrypt
Passwords must never be stored in plain text.
Use bcrypt for hashing.
Never expose passwords in API responses.
19. Database
MongoDB
MongoDB is selected because:
Flexible schema 
Easy integration 
Document-oriented storage 
Excellent for logistics-style applications 
Mongoose
Use Mongoose for:
Schema validation 
Models 
CRUD 
Relationships 
Query optimization 
20. Mapping Technology
Mapbox GL JS
Mapbox is mandatory.
Reasons:
Modern UI 
Interactive maps 
Smooth rendering 
Custom markers 
Route drawing 
No-fly zones 
The map is used in:
Route Optimization 
Delivery Tracking 
Mini Maps 
21. Version Control
Git
GitHub
Development should follow Git best practices.
Recommended branches:
main
development
feature/fleet
feature/routes
feature/delivery
feature/analytics
22. Project Architecture
The application follows a layered architecture.
┌────────────────────────────┐
│        React Frontend       │
└─────────────
┬
───────────────┘
              │
              │ Axios
              │
┌─────────────
▼
───────────────┐
│      Express REST API       │
└─────────────
┬
───────────────┘
              │
      Business Logic
              │
┌─────────────
▼
───────────────┐
│       DSA Layer             │
│ Arrays                      │
│ Sorting                     │
│ Searching                   │
│ Queue                       │
│ Priority Queue              │
│ Graph                       │
│ BFS                         │
│ Dijkstra                    │
└─────────────
┬
───────────────┘
              │
┌─────────────
▼
───────────────┐
│        MongoDB              │
└─────────────────────────────┘
23. Application Workflow
User
↓
React UI
↓
Axios Request
↓
Express API
↓
Authentication
↓
Controller
↓
Service Layer
↓
DSA Logic
↓
MongoDB
↓
Response
↓
React UI Update
Every request must pass through this flow.

Important:
 Keep DSA algorithms in a dedicated algorithms/ folder rather than embedding them directly in controllers. This makes the implementation easier to understand and demonstrate during your viva.
25. Development Principles
Every developer or AI must follow these principles:
Modularity
Each feature should be independent.
Reusability
Avoid duplicate components.
Readability
Prefer clear code over clever code.
Scalability
Future features should integrate without major restructuring.
Maintainability
Keep functions focused on a single responsibility.
26. Future-Proof Architecture
Although Version 1.0 excludes advanced animations and AI, the architecture should allow future additions without major refactoring.
Potential future enhancements:
GSAP page transitions 
Three.js drone visualization 
Real-time drone tracking 
Weather APIs 
Push notifications 
AI assistant 
Mobile application 
End of Section 3
Estimated formatted length:
 
12–15 pages
VOLUME 1
FOUNDATION & DEVELOPMENT GUIDELINES
SECTION 4
Complete Design System & UI Guidelines
27. Design Philosophy
The Drone Delivery Navigation System must not resemble a traditional admin panel or CRUD application. Instead, it should present itself as a 
premium Drone Command Center
 inspired by modern enterprise SaaS platforms.
The interface should feel:
Professional 
Minimal 
Futuristic 
Human-centered 
Spacious 
Easy to navigate 
Visually consistent 
The design language is inspired by:
Apple Human Interface Guidelines 
Linear 
Stripe Dashboard 
DJI Flight Control Software 
Notion 
Arc Browser 
The application should avoid excessive decoration, unnecessary gradients, or heavy visual effects. Elegance and clarity should take precedence over complexity.
28. Global UI Principles
The following principles are mandatory across the entire application:
Simplicity
Every screen should communicate one primary purpose.
Consistency
Navigation, spacing, typography, and interaction patterns must remain identical across all modules.
Spaciousness
The interface should never feel crowded. Use generous whitespace to separate sections and improve readability.
Hierarchy
The user's attention should naturally flow from the most important information to supporting content.
Visual Balance
No section should dominate the page unless intentionally designed as the hero section.
29. Visual Identity
The application follows a modern glassmorphism design system.
Core Characteristics
Frosted glass cards 
Soft ambient gradients 
Rounded corners 
Subtle shadows 
Thin borders 
Clean typography 
Light interface 
Premium illustrations 
Consistent iconography 
Avoid skeuomorphic effects or overly glossy elements.
30. Color System
Primary Dashboard Theme
The dashboard serves as the neutral command center.
Primary Colors:
White 
Soft Blue 
Light Gray 
Accent:
Soft Blue 
The dashboard must remain visually neutral because it is the entry point to all modules.
Module Identity System
Each module has its own accent color while preserving the same layout and design language.

<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="3A938008" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00353B92" w:rsidRPr="00353B92" w:rsidRDefault="00353B92" w:rsidP="00353B92"><w:r w:rsidRPr="00353B92"><w:t>Intelligence Center
Only the accent colors change. Layout, sidebar, header, typography, spacing, and components remain consistent.
Ambient Background Rule
The application should 
never
 replace the background entirely with a module color.
Instead:
Maintain a white/light base. 
Add a 
10–15% ambient tint
 using the module's accent color. 
Apply the accent to: 
Card highlights 
Hover states 
Buttons 
Charts 
Icons 
Progress indicators 
This creates module identity without sacrificing consistency.
31. Typography
Use a clean, modern sans-serif font throughout the application.
Recommended:
Inter 
SF Pro Display (if available) 
Poppins (fallback) 
Hierarchy
Page Title: 32–36px, Bold 
Section Title: 22–24px, Semi-bold 
Card Title: 18–20px, Medium 
Body Text: 15–16px 
Labels: 13–14px 
Captions: 12px 
Text colors:
Primary: Dark Gray 
Secondary: Medium Gray 
Muted: Light Gray 
Avoid excessive font weights or decorative fonts.
32. Glassmorphism Rules
Every primary panel should follow the same glass style.
Properties
Rounded corners: 24–32px 
Soft blur background 
Thin translucent border 
Gentle shadow 
High contrast text 
Light background transparency 
Cards should feel elevated but not floating excessively.
33. Layout System
All pages follow the same structural layout.
+------------------------------------------------------------+
| Header                                                     |
|------------------------------------------------------------|
| Sidebar | Main Content                                     |
|         |                                                  |
|         | Hero Section                                     |
|         |                                                  |
|         | Supporting Sections                             |
|         |                                                  |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
The sidebar remains fixed.
The main content scrolls independently.
34. Sidebar Design (LOCKED)
The sidebar is identical across all modules.
Do Not Change
Width 
Background color 
Icon style 
Hover effects 
Navigation spacing 
Logo placement 
Only the active module indicator changes.
35. Header Design (LOCKED)
Every module shares the same header layout.
Includes:
Module title 
Subtitle 
Companion drone 
Date 
Time 
Weather 
Notification icon 
User profile 
The companion drone always remains near the top center.
36. Card Design
Cards are the primary information containers.
Standard Card Rules
Rounded corners 
Glass background 
Soft shadow 
Thin border 
Hover lift effect 
Smooth transitions 
Internal padding 
Clear content hierarchy 
Cards should not appear flat or overly saturated.
37. Button Design
Buttons should follow one consistent style.
Primary Button
Filled with module accent color 
White text 
Rounded corners 
Soft shadow 
Secondary Button
White background 
Accent-colored border 
Accent-colored text 
Hover
Slight elevation 
Subtle scale 
Smooth color transition 
38. Form Elements
All forms should use the same design language.
Includes:
Rounded inputs 
Glass background 
Floating labels (optional) 
Focus ring using module accent color 
Clear validation messages 
Applies to:
Login 
Search 
Filters 
Forms 
39. Icons
Use a single icon library throughout the project.
Recommended:
Lucide React 
Icons should be:
Minimal 
Outline style 
Consistent stroke width 
Avoid mixing multiple icon sets.
40. Module Hero Sections
Every module has one primary focus.

The hero section should occupy the largest visual area.
41. Responsive Design
Desktop is the primary target.
Recommended breakpoints:
Desktop (≥1200px) 
Laptop (992–1199px) 
Tablet (768–991px) 
For tablets:
Sidebar becomes collapsible. 
Cards resize gracefully. 
Hero sections remain visible. 
Mobile support is optional for Version 1.0 but should not break the layout.
42. Visual Consistency Rules
The following elements must remain consistent:
Sidebar 
Header 
Typography 
Border radius 
Shadows 
Glass effects 
Card spacing 
Grid system 
Button style 
Icons 
Only the module accent color changes.
43. UI Lock Rules
The following decisions are 
locked
 and must not be changed during development:
Landing page layout 
Login page layout 
Dashboard structure 
Fleet Management layout 
Route Optimization layout 
Delivery Management layout 
Analytics Dashboard layout 
Sidebar design 
Header structure 
Module color identity 
Glassmorphism style 
Card orientation 
Navigation flow 
If changes are required in the future, they must be documented as a new version rather than modifying the existing specification.
End of Section 4
Estimated formatted length:
 
14–16 pages
VOLUME 1
FOUNDATION & DEVELOPMENT GUIDELINES
SECTION 5
React Component Architecture & Frontend Development Standards
44. Frontend Architecture Philosophy
The frontend must be designed around the following principles:
Component-first development
 – Every UI element should be a reusable React component. 
Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)
 – Each component should have one clear purpose. 
Separation of Concerns
 – UI, business logic, and API calls should remain separate. 
Type Safety
 – Every component and API interaction should use TypeScript interfaces. 
Scalability
 – New modules should integrate without restructuring the existing application. 
The goal is to produce a codebase that is clean, maintainable, and easy to extend.
45. Application Structure
The frontend follows a feature-based architecture.
src/
│
├
──
 assets/
│   
├
──
 images/
│   
├
──
 icons/
│   
├
──
 fonts/
│   └── illustrations/
│
├
──
 components/
│   
├
──
 common/
│   
├
──
 dashboard/
│   
├
──
 fleet/
│   
├
──
 route/
│   
├
──
 delivery/
│   └── analytics/
│
├
──
 pages/
│
├
──
 layouts/
│
├
──
 hooks/
│
├
──
 services/
│
├
──
 interfaces/
│
├
──
 context/
│
├
──
 utils/
│
├
──
 routes/
│
├
──
 App.tsx
│
└── main.tsx
Every folder has a single responsibility.
46. Page Architecture
Every major screen is a 
page
.
Required pages:
Landing 
Login 
Dashboard 
Fleet Management 
Route Optimization 
Delivery Management 
Analytics Dashboard 
Each page should be responsible only for layout and composition.
Business logic belongs elsewhere.
47. Layout Architecture
Layouts define shared page structure.
Example:
MainLayout
Header
Sidebar
Main Content
Footer (optional)
The Dashboard and all modules reuse the same MainLayout.
The Login and Landing pages use separate layouts.
48. Component Hierarchy
Each page should be composed of small reusable components.
Example:
Dashboard
├
──
 Header
├
──
 Sidebar
├
──
 WelcomeCard
├
──
 ModuleCard
├
──
 WeatherWidget
├
──
 TimeWidget
├
──
 ProfileCard
├
──
 NotificationButton
├
──
 FloatingDrone
Every component should be independently reusable.
49. Shared Components
These components should exist only once.
Examples:
Button
Card
Modal
Input
SearchBar
Dropdown
Badge
Avatar
Loader
ConfirmationDialog
Tooltip
EmptyState
Pagination
These should be reusable across all modules.
50. Feature Components
Each module owns its own components.
Fleet:
DroneCard
FleetStats
DroneDetails
BatteryIndicator
MissionCard
Route:
MapContainer
WarehouseSelector
DestinationSelector
RouteCard
NoFlyZoneLayer
RouteLegend
Delivery:
DeliveryCard
Timeline
TrackingPanel
PackageCard
AssignmentPanel
Analytics:
KpiCard
ChartCard
Heatmap
ReportCard
RankingCard
PredictionCard
51. Component Naming Convention
Use PascalCase.
Examples:
DroneCard.tsx
RouteMap.tsx
AnalyticsPanel.tsx
FleetSidebar.tsx
Never use vague names such as:
Component1
CardNew
TestPage
52. Folder Naming Convention
Folders should use lowercase.
Example:
fleet
delivery
analytics
components
hooks
services
Avoid spaces.
53. State Management Strategy
Use React's built-in features first.
Preferred order:
Local State (useState) 
Shared Context (Context API) 
Custom Hooks 
Avoid introducing global state libraries unless absolutely necessary.
54. Custom Hooks
Extract reusable logic into hooks.
Examples:
useAuth
useFleet
useRoutes
useDeliveries
useAnalytics
useMapbox
Hooks should never render UI.
55. TypeScript Interfaces
Every data model must have a dedicated interface.
Examples:
interface Drone {}
interface Delivery {}
interface Warehouse {}
interface Route {}
interface User {}
interface Analytics {}
Never use anonymous object types repeatedly.
56. API Layer
API communication belongs inside the services/ folder.
Example:
services/
authService.ts
fleetService.ts
routeService.ts
deliveryService.ts
analyticsService.ts
Pages and components should never call Axios directly.
57. Routing Standards
Use React Router.
Suggested routes:
/
login
dashboard
fleet
route
delivery
analytics
Protect private routes using JWT authentication.
58. Forms
Forms should follow one structure.
Validation
↓
Error Messages
↓
Submit
↓
Loading State
↓
Success/Error Feedback
Never allow duplicate submissions.
59. Loading States
Every page that fetches data must include:
Skeleton loaders 
Loading spinner (only where appropriate) 
Empty state 
Error state 
Avoid blank screens.
60. Error Handling
Every API request should handle:
Network errors 
Unauthorized access 
Validation errors 
Server errors 
Display user-friendly messages.
61. Responsive Standards
Desktop-first design.
Cards should rearrange gracefully on smaller screens.
Sidebar collapses on tablets.
No horizontal scrolling.
62. Code Style Guidelines
Prefer functional components. 
Use arrow functions. 
Keep components under ~250 lines where practical. 
Extract repeated logic. 
Avoid deeply nested JSX. 
Keep props minimal. 
63. Performance Guidelines
Lazy load pages. 
Memoize expensive calculations when needed. 
Avoid unnecessary re-renders. 
Use pagination for large datasets. 
Reuse components instead of duplicating them. 
64. Accessibility
Every interactive element should have:
Accessible labels 
Keyboard navigation 
Visible focus state 
Sufficient color contrast 
Semantic HTML should be preferred over generic <div> elements whenever appropriate.
65. Frontend Quality Checklist
Before considering a page complete, verify:
Uses reusable components. 
Uses TypeScript interfaces. 
Follows the design system. 
Is responsive. 
Handles loading and error states. 
Does not duplicate logic. 
Uses centralized API services. 
Passes linting and formatting. 
66. Locked Frontend Rules
The following rules are mandatory:
Use React + TypeScript. 
Use Tailwind CSS. 
Keep the sidebar identical across all modules. 
Reuse common components. 
Do not hardcode API URLs in components. 
Keep business logic out of UI components. 
Follow the approved routing structure. 
Preserve the locked UI layouts and module identities. 
End of Section 5
Estimated formatted length:
 
14–16 pages
VOLUME 1
FOUNDATION & DEVELOPMENT GUIDELINES
SECTION 6
Backend Architecture & API Development Standards
67. Backend Philosophy
The backend is the 
brain
 of the Drone Delivery Navigation System.
Its responsibilities are to:
Authenticate users. 
Handle CRUD operations. 
Execute DSA algorithms. 
Communicate with MongoDB. 
Serve data through REST APIs. 
Maintain security and validation. 
The backend 
must not
 contain UI-related logic.
68. Backend Technology Stack
The backend must use only the approved technologies.

No additional backend frameworks should be introduced unless approved.
69. Backend Folder Structure
The backend should follow a modular architecture.
server/
│
├
──
 src/
│   
├
──
 config/
│   │   
├
──
 db.ts
│   │   
├
──
 env.ts
│   │   └── mapbox.ts
│   │
│   
├
──
 controllers/
│   │
│   
├
──
 services/
│   │
│   
├
──
 routes/
│   │
│   
├
──
 middleware/
│   │
│   
├
──
 models/
│   │
│   
├
──
 algorithms/
│   │   
├
──
 graph/
│   │   
├
──
 queue/
│   │

──
 priorityQueue/
│   │

 sorting/

 app.ts

package.json
│
└── 
tsconfig.json
70. Layered Architecture
Every request must follow the same path.
Client
↓
Route
↓
Middleware
↓
Controller
↓
Service
↓
DSA Algorithm (if required)
↓
MongoDB
↓
Response
Controllers should remain lightweight.
Business logic belongs in the Service layer.
Algorithms belong in the algorithms/ folder.
71. Route Organization
Routes should be grouped by feature.
/
auth
/
fleet
/
routes
/
deliveries
/
analytics
/
users
Each route file should define only the endpoints for its feature.
72. Controller Responsibilities
Controllers should:
Receive HTTP requests. 
Validate incoming data. 
Call the appropriate service. 
Return standardized responses. 
Controllers 
must not
:
Contain DSA implementations. 
Perform database queries directly. 
Contain business logic. 
73. Service Layer Responsibilities
The Service layer is responsible for:
Business logic. 
Database interaction. 
Calling DSA algorithms. 
Data transformations. 
Validation beyond request-level checks. 
Example:
FleetController
↓
FleetService
↓
Priority Queue
↓
Drone Assignment
74. DSA Layer
The project is a 
DSA course 
project
,
 therefore algorithms must remain isolated and reusable.
Example structure:
algorithms/
searching/
sorting/
graph/
queue/
priorityQueue/
analytics/
Each algorithm should expose reusable functions.
Example:
findDroneById(
)
sortByBattery(
)
assignDrone(
)
findShortestRoute(
)
calculateAnalytics(
)
75. MongoDB Collections
The backend should manage the following collections.
Users
Stores authentication data.
Fields:
Name 
Email 
Password (hashed) 
Role 
Drones
Stores drone information.
Fields:
Drone ID 
Model 
Status 
Battery 
Speed 
Capacity 
Current Location 
Warehouses
Stores warehouse locations.
Fields:
Name 
Coordinates 
Capacity 
Deliveries
Stores delivery records.
Fields:
Package ID 
Customer 
Status 
Assigned Drone 
Priority 
ETA 
Destination 
Routes
Stores calculated routes.
Fields:
Start 
End 
Distance 
Estimated Time 
Algorithm Used 
Analytics
Stores aggregated statistics.
Fields:
Date 
Total Deliveries 
Success Rate 
Average Time 
Distance Covered 
76. API Design Standards
All APIs must follow REST principles.
Naming examples:
GET     /fleet
GET     /fleet/:id
POST    /fleet
PUT     /fleet/:id
DELETE  /
fleet/:id
Avoid verbs in endpoint names.
Use nouns instead.
77. Standard API Response Format
Every response should follow the same structure.
Success
{
  "success": true,
  "message": "Drone retrieved successfully.",
  "data": {}
}
Error
{
  "success": false,
  "message": "Drone not found.",
  "error": {}
}
This consistency simplifies frontend integration.
78. Authentication Flow
Authentication process:
Login Request
↓
Validate User
↓
Compare Password
↓
Generate JWT
↓
Return Token
↓
Store Token (Client)
↓
Access Protected Routes
Protected endpoints must verify JWT before executing.
79. Middleware
Required middleware:
Authentication 
Error Handler 
Request Logger 
Input Validation 
CORS 
Responsibilities:
Authenticate users. 
Handle errors. 
Validate requests. 
Log important events. 
80. Password Security
Passwords must:
Be hashed using bcrypt. 
Never be stored in plain text. 
Never be returned in API responses. 
81. Error Handling
The backend must gracefully handle:
Validation failures 
Unauthorized access 
Missing resources 
Duplicate data 
Internal server errors 
Return meaningful HTTP status codes and descriptive messages.
82. Logging
Important events should be logged.
Examples:
User login 
Failed authentication 
Delivery creation 
Route generation 
Drone assignment 
Logs should aid debugging without exposing sensitive information.
83. Environment Variables
Sensitive configuration must be stored 
in .env
.
Examples:
PORT
MONGODB_URI
JWT_SECRET
MAPBOX_ACCESS_TOKEN
Never hardcode secrets into the source code.
84. Backend Coding Standards
Use TypeScript. 
Use async/await instead of nested callbacks. 
Keep functions small and focused. 
Avoid duplicated code. 
Use meaningful names. 
Separate concerns across layers. 
85. Backend Quality Checklist
Before considering the backend complete, verify:
RESTful endpoints implemented. 
JWT authentication working. 
CRUD operations functional. 
MongoDB integration complete. 
DSA algorithms isolated. 
Validation implemented. 
Errors handled consistently. 
Environment variables configured. 
Code passes linting and formatting. 
86. Locked Backend Rules
The following decisions are locked:
Node.js + Express.js backend. 
MongoDB with Mongoose. 
Layered architecture. 
Dedicated algorithms/ folder. 
JWT authentication. 
REST API design. 
Standard response format. 
Modular folder structure. 
Business logic in services only. 
Controllers remain thin. 
Real DSA implementations only. 
These rules must be followed throughout development.
End of Section 6
Estimated formatted length:
 
15–18 pages
VOLUME 1
FOUNDATION & DEVELOPMENT GUIDELINES
SECTION 7
Database Design & DSA Architecture
87. Database Philosophy
The Drone Delivery Navigation System is built on 
MongoDB
, a document-oriented NoSQL database. The database design follows the principles of 
modularity
, 
normalization where appropriate
, and 
efficient querying
 while supporting the application's DSA-focused operations.
The database should:
Store structured operational data. 
Support efficient CRUD operations. 
Minimize redundant data. 
Enable algorithm execution with minimal preprocessing. 
Remain extensible for future enhancements. 
88. Database Overview
The system consists of six primary collections:
Users
│
├
──
 Authentication
Drones
│
├
──
 Fleet Information
Warehouses
│
├
──
 Warehouse Locations
Deliveries
│
├
──
 Delivery Missions
Routes
│
├
──
 Route Information
Analytics
│
└── Aggregated Statistics
Each collection serves a distinct business function and maps directly to one or more application modules.
89. Collection Design
Users Collection
Purpose:
Store user authentication and authorization details.
Fields:
_id
name
email
password (hashed)
role
createdAt
updatedAt
Role values:
Administrator 
Fleet Manager 
Logistics Operator 
Indexes:
email (Unique) 
Drones Collection
Purpose:
Store complete fleet information.
Fields:
_id
droneId
model
batteryLevel
status
currentLocation
maximumSpeed
payloadCapacity
healthStatus
lastMaintenance
createdAt
updatedAt
Status values:
Available 
Busy 
Charging 
Maintenance 
Indexes:
droneId 
status 
Warehouses Collection
Purpose:
Store warehouse locations.
Fields:
_id
warehouseName
latitude
longitude
address
maximumCapacity
currentStock
Indexes:
warehouseName 
Deliveries Collection
Purpose:
Manage delivery missions.
Fields:
_id
packageId
customerName
destination
priority
status
assignedDrone
estimatedTime
distance
createdAt
completedAt
Priority:
High 
Medium 
Low 
Status:
Pending 
Assigned 
In Transit 
Delivered 
Cancelled 
Indexes:
packageId 
status 
Routes Collection
Purpose:
Store optimized routes.
Fields:
_id
warehouse
destination
algorithm
distance
estimatedTime
visitedNodes
routeCoordinates
Indexes:
warehouse 
destination 
Analytics Collection
Purpose:
Store summarized operational data.
Fields:
_id
date
deliveriesCompleted
averageTime
distanceCovered
batteryUsage
successRate
Indexes:
date 
90. Database Relationships
Although MongoDB is document-oriented, logical relationships exist.
Users
↓
Manage
↓
Fleet
↓
Assigned
↓
Deliveries
↓
Uses
↓
Routes
↓
Generates
↓
Analytics
Relationships should be represented through ObjectId references where appropriate.
91. CRUD Mapping
Each module supports CRUD operations where applicable.
Fleet
Create Drone
Read Drone
Update Drone
Delete Drone
Delivery
Create Delivery
Read Delivery
Update Delivery
Delete Delivery
Warehouses
Create
Read
Update
Delete
Analytics data is primarily generated rather than manually modified.
92. DSA Philosophy
The project is designed to demonstrate how classical Data Structures and Algorithms solve practical logistics problems.
Every algorithm must power an actual feature visible in the application.
No algorithm should exist merely for academic documentation.
93. DSA Mapping

<w:trPr><w:tblCellSpacing w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/></w:trPr><w:tc><w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="03EB3F9E" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00353B92" w:rsidRPr="00353B92" w:rsidRDefault="00353B92" w:rsidP="00353B92"><w:r w:rsidRPr="00353B92"><w:t>Analytics
<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="72F43ACE" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00353B92" w:rsidRPr="00353B92" w:rsidRDefault="00353B92" w:rsidP="00353B92"><w:r w:rsidRPr="00353B92"><w:t>Sorting
<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="1E80E9B3" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00353B92" w:rsidRPr="00353B92" w:rsidRDefault="00353B92" w:rsidP="00353B92"><w:r w:rsidRPr="00353B92"><w:t>Rankings
94. Fleet DSA Design
Fleet data is maintained as an array of drone objects.
Example:
Drone[
]
↓
Search
↓
Sort
↓
Assign
Searching
Used when:
Find drone by ID 
Find drone by model 
Find drone by status 
Sorting
Sort by:
Battery 
Speed 
Capacity 
Health 
Priority Queue
Assignment priority is determined using:
Availability 
Battery Level 
Distance from Warehouse 
Health Status 
This demonstrates practical Priority Queue usage.
95. Route Optimization DSA Design
The city is represented as a weighted graph.
Warehouse A
↓
Intersection
↓
Intersection
↓
Customer
Nodes:
Warehouses 
Intersections 
Delivery Points 
Edges:
Roads 
Weights:
Distance 
Dijkstra
Purpose:
Find shortest delivery route.
BFS
Purpose:
Graph traversal and reachability.
Future upgrades may include A* without changing the architecture.
96. Delivery DSA Design
Deliveries are maintained in a queue.
Pending
↓
Assigned
↓
In Transit
↓
Delivered
Queue
FIFO scheduling.
Priority Queue
Emergency deliveries bypass the normal queue.
Priority:
High
↓
Medium
↓
Low
97. Analytics DSA Design
Analytics primarily demonstrate:
Arrays
Hash Maps
Sorting
Searching
Aggregation
Example:
Drone Performance
↓
Sort
↓
Top Performing Drone
↓
Display Ranking
98. Time Complexity Considerations
The project should explain the complexity of its algorithms during documentation and viva.

(V + E) log V) with Priority Queue
The application does not need to display these values to users, but the implementation should reflect these choices.
99. Why These Algorithms?
The selected algorithms align naturally with the problem domain:
Arrays efficiently store collections of drones and deliveries. 
Searching algorithms locate specific records. 
Sorting ranks drones and analytics. 
Queues model delivery processing. 
Priority Queues optimize mission assignment. 
Graphs represent the road network. 
Dijkstra computes optimal routes. 
BFS demonstrates graph traversal. 
Hash Maps efficiently aggregate analytics. 
This ensures the DSA concepts are meaningful rather than artificial.
100. Future Scalability
The DSA layer should be designed so additional algorithms can be introduced later without affecting existing modules.
Examples:
A* Search 
Bellman–Ford 
Floyd–Warshall 
Kruskal's Algorithm 
Prim's Algorithm 
These enhancements belong to future versions and should not alter the Version 1.0 architecture.
101. Locked DSA Rules
The following rules are mandatory:
Every algorithm must have a visible application feature. 
Algorithms must be implemented in the dedicated algorithms/ directory. 
Controllers must never contain algorithm logic. 
Services invoke algorithms. 
Algorithms remain reusable and independently testable. 
Time complexity should be documented for academic explanation. 
Do not replace the approved algorithms without updating the specification. 
End of Section 7
Estimated formatted length:
 
18–20 pages
VOLUME 1
FOUNDATION & DEVELOPMENT GUIDELINES
SECTION 8
Development Roadmap, Coding Standards & AI Development Instructions
102. Development Philosophy
The Drone Delivery Navigation System must be developed using an 
incremental, module-driven approach
.
The application should never be built all at once. Each module should be completed, tested, and integrated before moving to the next.
Development priorities:
Stability 
Correctness 
DSA Implementation 
Clean UI 
Performance 
Future Scalability 
103. Development Phases
The project should be built in the following order.
Phase 1 – Project Setup
Objectives:
Create React + TypeScript project 
Configure Tailwind CSS 
Configure Node.js + Express 
Configure MongoDB 
Initialize Git repository 
Create folder structure 
Configure environment variables 
Deliverables:
Running frontend 
Running backend 
Database connection 
Initial Git commit 
Phase 2 – Authentication
Build:
Login page 
JWT authentication 
Password hashing 
Protected routes 
Logout functionality 
Deliverables:
Secure login flow 
Token validation 
Session persistence 
Phase 3 – Dashboard
Build:
Main dashboard 
Sidebar 
Header 
Module navigation 
Dashboard cards 
Deliverables:
Complete command center 
Navigation between modules 
Phase 4 – Fleet Management
Implement:
Drone CRUD 
Search 
Sorting 
Priority Queue 
Fleet statistics 
Deliverables:
Functional Fleet Management module 
DSA integration 
Phase 5 – Route Optimization
Implement:
Mapbox GL JS 
Graph representation 
Dijkstra 
BFS 
Route visualization 
Warehouse selection 
Deliverables:
Interactive route planning 
Phase 6 – Delivery Management
Implement:
Delivery CRUD 
Queue 
Priority Queue 
Assignment workflow 
Delivery tracking 
Deliverables:
Complete delivery lifecycle 
Phase 7 – Analytics Dashboard
Implement:
KPI cards 
Charts 
Heatmap 
Reports 
Rankings 
Statistics 
Deliverables:
Executive analytics interface 
Phase 8 – Integration & Testing
Tasks:
Connect frontend to backend 
Verify authentication 
Validate CRUD operations 
Test DSA implementations 
Optimize performance 
Resolve bugs 
104. Git Workflow
Development should use Git throughout.
Recommended branches:
main
development
feature/auth
feature/dashboard
feature/fleet
feature/routes
feature/delivery
feature/analytics
Guidelines:
Develop features in separate branches. 
Merge only after testing. 
Keep commits small and meaningful. 
Commit message examples:
feat: add fleet management page 
fix: resolve route calculation bug 
refactor: optimize delivery service 
docs: update README 
105. Coding Standards
General
Use TypeScript everywhere. 
Write self-explanatory code. 
Keep functions focused. 
Avoid duplicate logic. 
Prefer composition over inheritance. 
Naming Conventions
Variables
Use camelCase.
Examples:
batteryLevel
deliveryStatus
currentDrone
Components
Use PascalCase.
Examples:
DroneCard
FleetTable
AnalyticsChart
Files
React components:
PascalCase
Utility files:
camelCase
Folders:
lowercase
Functions
Use verbs.
Examples:
calculateRoute(
)
assignDrone(
)
sortFleet(
)
generateReport(
)
106. Commenting Guidelines
Use comments to explain 
why
, not 
what
.
Good:
// Priority Queue ensures urgent deliveries are assigned first.
Avoid:
// Increment i
The code should be self-explanatory wherever possible.
107. Code Quality
Every feature must satisfy:
Type-safe 
Readable 
Reusable 
Testable 
Modular 
No duplicated business logic.
108. Error Handling Strategy
The application must gracefully handle:
Authentication Errors
Invalid credentials 
Expired token 
Unauthorized access 
Validation Errors
Missing fields 
Invalid data 
Duplicate records 
System Errors
Database unavailable 
API failures 
Network interruptions 
Every error should provide a user-friendly message.
109. Testing Strategy
Testing should cover:
Frontend
Page rendering 
Form validation 
Navigation 
API integration 
Backend
CRUD operations 
JWT authentication 
Validation 
Database queries 
DSA logic 
Integration
Login flow 
Module navigation 
Route generation 
Delivery assignment 
Analytics updates 
110. Performance Guidelines
The application should:
Lazy-load routes. 
Minimize unnecessary renders. 
Use pagination for large lists. 
Avoid redundant API requests. 
Keep bundle size reasonable. 
Performance should never come at the expense of code readability.
111. Security Guidelines
Mandatory practices:
Hash passwords with bcrypt. 
Protect private routes with JWT. 
Validate all user input. 
Never expose sensitive data. 
Store secrets in environment variables. 
Avoid trusting client-side data. 
112. Documentation Requirements
The project must include:
README 
Installation guide 
Folder structure overview 
API documentation 
Database description 
DSA explanation 
Technology stack 
Setup instructions 
Future documents (outside Volume 1) will expand these sections.
113. AI Developer Instructions
This project is intended to be developed with assistance from AI coding tools.
The AI must follow these rules:
Do 
not
 redesign the approved UI. 
Build pages according to the locked layouts. 
Use the approved technology stack only. 
Implement real DSA algorithms, not placeholder code. 
Keep React components reusable and modular. 
Maintain the fixed sidebar and header across modules. 
Use Mapbox GL JS for all mapping features. 
Follow the folder structures defined in this specification. 
Keep backend logic inside services. 
Keep algorithms isolated in the algorithms/ directory. 
Produce production-quality, well-commented, type-safe code. 
Do not introduce unnecessary libraries or frameworks. 
114. Definition of Done (DoD)
A feature is considered complete only when:
UI matches the approved design. 
Responsive behavior is verified. 
Backend APIs function correctly. 
MongoDB integration works. 
DSA implementation is complete. 
Loading and error states are handled. 
Code passes linting. 
No critical bugs remain. 
Documentation is updated. 
115. Acceptance Criteria
The overall project is accepted when:
Landing page functions. 
Login authentication is secure. 
Dashboard navigates correctly. 
Fleet Management operates correctly. 
Route Optimization generates routes using Dijkstra. 
Delivery Management demonstrates Queue and Priority Queue behavior. 
Analytics Dashboard displays meaningful insights. 
All CRUD operations are functional. 
JWT authentication protects private pages. 
MongoDB stores and retrieves data correctly. 
The application remains visually consistent with the locked design system. 
116. Future Enhancements (Post-Submission)
The following features are intentionally excluded from Version 1.0 but planned for Version 2.0:
GSAP page transitions 
Three.js drone rendering 
Cinematic drone navigation 
Advanced micro-interactions 
Real-time weather integration 
Live drone telemetry 
Push notifications 
AI assistant 
Mobile application 
Advanced analytics and forecasting 
The architecture defined in Volume 1 should support these upgrades without requiring major restructuring.
117. Final Non-Negotiable Rules
The following decisions are 
locked
:
Technology
React + TypeScript 
Tailwind CSS 
Node.js 
Express.js 
MongoDB + Mongoose 
JWT 
Mapbox GL JS 
Design
Landing Page (Locked) 
Login Page (Locked) 
Dashboard (Locked) 
Fleet Management (Locked) 
Route Optimization (Locked) 
Delivery Management (Locked) 
Analytics Dashboard (Locked) 
UI
Glassmorphism 
Fixed Sidebar 
Fixed Header 
Module-specific accent colors 
Consistent typography and spacing 
DSA
Arrays 
Searching 
Binary Search 
Merge Sort / Quick Sort 
Queue 
Priority Queue 
Graph 
BFS 
Dijkstra 
Hash Maps 
Data Aggregation 
No substitutions should be made without updating the specification.
118. Volume 1 Completion Statement
This concludes 
Volume 1 – Foundation & Development Guidelines
.
Volume 1 establishes:
The vision and purpose of the project. 
The approved technology stack. 
The software architecture. 
The frontend and backend engineering standards. 
The database and DSA architecture. 
The design system. 
The development roadmap. 
The quality standards and rules. 
All future volumes must build upon these foundations and must not contradict any locked decisions defined herein.
End of Volume 1
Estimated formatted length:
 
18–20 pages
DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 2 – UI/UX & FRONTEND SPECIFICATION
SECTION 1
Introduction, Navigation Flow & User Journey
Cover Statement
This volume defines the 
entire frontend experience
 of the Drone Delivery Navigation System.
Unlike Volume 1, which focused on architecture and technologies, Volume 2 focuses on 
how users interact with the application
, 
how every page is structured
, and 
how navigation flows throughout the system
.
Every future frontend page, component, animation, and interaction must follow the specifications defined in this volume.
This document acts as the 
official UI/UX specification
 for the project.
1. Purpose of Volume 2
The purpose of this volume is to ensure that every screen of the application follows one consistent design language.
This document specifies:
Complete user flow 
Screen hierarchy 
Navigation rules 
Layout consistency 
Frontend interaction standards 
Responsive behavior 
User experience guidelines 
Component relationships 
This volume does 
not
 describe backend logic. Backend implementation is documented in later volumes.
2. User Experience Philosophy
The Drone Delivery Navigation System is designed to feel like a 
premium Drone Command Center
, not an ordinary admin dashboard.
The user should experience:
Clean interface 
Fast navigation 
Minimal distractions 
Clear hierarchy 
Professional aesthetics 
Smooth workflows 
Predictable interactions 
The interface should always communicate confidence and precision.
3. User Journey
The application follows a guided journey from introduction to operational control.
Visitor
   │
   
▼
Landing Page
   │
Click "Get Started"
   │
   
▼
Login Page
   │
Successful Authentication
   │
   
▼
Dashboard
   │
Choose Module
   
├
──────────────
┬
──────────────
┬
──────────────┐
   
▼
              
▼
              
▼
              
▼
Fleet        Route         Delivery      Analytics
Management   
Optimization  Management
    Dashboard
The flow is intentionally linear and intuitive.
4. Navigation Philosophy
Navigation must always satisfy three principles:
Simplicity
Users should never wonder where to go next.
Consistency
The same navigation behavior should exist across every page.
Predictability
Every button, icon, and menu should behave consistently throughout the application.
5. Application Sitemap
/
│
├
──
 Landing Page
│
├
──
 Login
│
└── Dashboard
      │
      
├
──
 Fleet Management
      │
      
├
──
 Route Optimization
      │
      
├
──
 Delivery Management
      │
      └── Analytics Dashboard
No additional pages are required for Version 1.0 unless directly supporting these modules (e.g., profile or settings).
6. Screen Hierarchy
The importance of each screen is defined below.

The Dashboard acts as the central hub.
7. Navigation Rules
The following rules are mandatory.
Landing Page
Allowed Navigation:
Login 
Explore Features (scroll) 
No authenticated content.
Login Page
Allowed Navigation:
Login 
Return to Landing 
No dashboard access without authentication.
Dashboard
Navigation:
Fleet Management 
Route Optimization 
Delivery Management 
Analytics Dashboard 
Sidebar remains visible.
Module Pages
Each module:
Uses the same sidebar. 
Uses the same header. 
Allows navigation to any other module. 
Returns to Dashboard via sidebar. 
8. Authentication Flow
Landing Page
↓
Login Page
↓
Enter Credentials
↓
Validate User
↓
JWT Generated
↓
Dashboard
↓
Protected Modules
If authentication fails:
Display a clear error message. 
Remain on the Login page. 
Preserve entered email (optional). 
Clear password field for security. 
9. Protected Route Rules
The following pages require authentication:
Dashboard 
Fleet Management 
Route Optimization 
Delivery Management 
Analytics Dashboard 
If the token is missing or invalid:
User Requests Protected Page
↓
JWT Validation
↓
Invalid
↓
Redirect to Login
10. Global Layout Rules
Every authenticated page follows the same structure.
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
|                       HEADER                              |
+-----------+-----------------------------------------------+
|           |                                               |
| SIDEBAR   |              MAIN CONTENT                     |
|           |                                               |
|           |                                               |
|           |                                               |
+-----------+-----------------------------------------------+
The sidebar remains fixed.
The content area scrolls independently.
11. Sidebar Navigation Rules
The sidebar is 
locked
 and must remain identical throughout the application.
Contents
Logo 
Dashboard 
Fleet Management 
Route Optimization 
Delivery Management 
Analytics Dashboard 
Logout 
Behavior
Active module highlighted. 
Smooth hover effect. 
No layout changes. 
No module-specific recoloring. 
12. Header Navigation Rules
The header remains consistent across all authenticated pages.
It includes:
Page title 
Page subtitle 
Companion drone (locked visual element) 
Current date 
Current time 
Weather widget 
Notification icon 
User profile avatar 
Only the page title and subtitle change depending on the current module.
13. Page Transition Rules
Version 1.0 uses simple transitions only.
Allowed:
Fade in 
Fade out 
Slight scale on cards 
Hover animations 
Smooth scrolling 
Not allowed in Version 1.0:
GSAP 
Three.js 
Complex cinematic animations 
These are reserved for Version 2.0.
14. Navigation Feedback
Users should always know where they are.
The UI must provide:
Active sidebar highlight 
Clear page title 
Module accent color 
Breadcrumbs (optional) 
Hover feedback 
Click feedback 
Loading indicator during navigation 
15. Interaction Principles
Every interaction should feel:
Immediate 
Responsive 
Smooth 
Intentional 
Avoid:
Delayed responses 
Abrupt UI changes 
Unexpected navigation 
16. Empty States
If a page contains no data, display meaningful placeholders.
Examples:
Fleet:
"No drones available."
Delivery:
"No active deliveries."
Analytics:
"No analytics data yet."
Route:
"Select a warehouse and destination to generate a route."
Avoid blank pages.
17. Loading States
Every data-fetching page must include loading indicators.
Preferred order:
Skeleton loaders 
Placeholder cards 
Spinner (only when appropriate) 
Never leave the interface blank while waiting for data.
18. Error States
When an error occurs:
Explain what happened. 
Suggest a corrective action. 
Allow retry. 
Example:
"Unable to load fleet data. Please check your connection and try again."
Avoid technical jargon for end users.
19. Accessibility Guidelines
The frontend should support:
Keyboard navigation 
Visible focus indicators 
Semantic HTML 
Sufficient color contrast 
Accessible labels for forms and buttons 
Accessibility should be considered from the beginning rather than added later.
20. Success Criteria for Volume 2
The UI implementation is considered successful when:
Navigation is intuitive. 
The user journey is seamless. 
Every page follows the locked design language. 
Layout consistency is maintained. 
Protected routes function correctly. 
Loading, empty, and error states are implemented. 
Responsive behavior works as specified. 
21. Locked Rules for Section 1
The following decisions are 
locked
:
Landing → Login → Dashboard flow. 
Dashboard is the central navigation hub. 
Sidebar remains identical across all authenticated pages. 
Header remains consistent across modules. 
Module navigation occurs through the sidebar. 
No unauthorized access to protected pages. 
Simple transitions only in Version 1.0. 
Future animation upgrades will be documented separately. 
End of Volume 2 – Section 1
Estimated formatted length:
 
10–12 pages
DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 2 – UI/UX & FRONTEND SPECIFICATION
SECTION 2
Landing Page Specification
22. Landing Page Purpose
The Landing Page is the 
first impression
 of the Drone Delivery Navigation System.
Its purpose is 
not
 to function as a traditional marketing website. Instead, it introduces the platform as a futuristic Drone Command Center and guides users toward authentication.
The Landing Page should immediately communicate:
Professionalism 
Innovation 
Reliability 
Modern technology 
Premium software quality 
The design should create excitement while remaining clean and minimal.
23. Primary Objectives
The Landing Page must achieve the following objectives:
Introduce the Drone Delivery Navigation System. 
Explain the application's purpose. 
Showcase the four core modules. 
Build user confidence through a polished interface. 
Encourage users to log in. 
Establish the visual identity carried throughout the application. 
24. User Journey
The Landing Page follows a simple user flow:
Visitor
   │
   
▼
Landing Page
   │
   
├
──
 Explore Features
   │
   
├
──
 Scroll Through Sections
   │
   └── Click "Get Started"
            │
            
▼
       Login Page
No additional navigation paths should distract the user.
25. Overall Layout
The Landing Page is divided into 
five major sections
:
------------------------------------------------------
Navigation Bar
------------------------------------------------------
Hero Section
------------------------------------------------------
Core Features Section
------------------------------------------------------
Why Choose Our System Section
------------------------------------------------------
Footer
------------------------------------------------------
Each section occupies the full width of the viewport with generous spacing.
26. Navigation Bar
Purpose
Provide minimal navigation while maintaining focus on the primary call-to-action.
Layout
---------------------------------------------------------
Logo
                Navigation Links
                              Get Started Button
---------------------------------------------------------
Left Side
Application Logo
Text:
Drone Navigation System
Center
Navigation Links:
Home 
Features 
About 
Right Side
Primary Button:
Get Started
Clicking this button navigates to the Login Page.
27. Hero Section
The Hero Section is the visual centerpiece of the Landing Page.
Layout
------------------------------------------------------------
                HEADER
------------------------------------------------------------
LEFT SIDE                 RIGHT SIDE
Project Title             Premium Drone Illustration
Description               Floating Elements
Buttons                   Ambient Effects
------------------------------------------------------------
Left Content
Main Heading:
Drone Delivery Navigation System
Subheading:
"Intelligent Fleet Management Powered by Data Structures & Algorithms."
Description:
A short paragraph describing how the system enables efficient fleet management, intelligent route planning, delivery tracking, and operational analytics.
Buttons:
Get Started 
Learn More 
Right Content
A premium drone illustration floating above a futuristic city skyline.
The illustration should include:
Soft shadows 
Ambient lighting 
Minimal floating motion (future enhancement) 
Glassmorphism elements 
Smooth gradients 
The drone should be the focal visual element.
28. Hero Background
The background should remain minimal.
Requirements:
Soft blue gradient 
Light city skyline silhouette 
Ambient circular glow behind the drone 
Subtle grid or abstract pattern 
No visual clutter 
Avoid overly dark or neon themes.
29. Feature Showcase
Immediately below the Hero Section.
Purpose:
Introduce the four core modules.
Layout:
------------------------------------------------------
Fleet Management
Route Optimization
Delivery Management
Analytics Dashboard
------------------------------------------------------
Each feature is represented by:
Large icon 
Title 
Short description 
Hover effect:
Slight lift 
Accent glow 
Shadow enhancement 
30. Why Choose Our System
A section highlighting the project's strengths.
Suggested points:
Practical DSA implementation 
Real-world logistics simulation 
Modern UI/UX 
Secure authentication 
Interactive maps 
Modular architecture 
Displayed as elegant glass cards.
31. Footer
Minimal footer containing:
Project Name 
Developer Information (optional) 
GitHub link (future) 
Version number 
Copyright 
The footer should not dominate the page.
32. Color Palette
Primary Colors:
White 
Soft Blue 
Light Gray 
Accent Color:
Electric Blue 
Background:
Very light gradient with subtle blue tint.
No heavy saturation.
33. Typography
Use the typography system defined in Volume 1.
Hero Heading:
Large, bold, highly readable.
Subheading:
Medium weight.
Descriptions:
Comfortable line spacing.
Buttons:
Bold, concise text.
34. Spacing Guidelines
Maintain generous whitespace.
Recommended spacing:
80–120px between major sections. 
32–48px between elements within a section. 
Consistent horizontal padding. 
The interface should never feel crowded.
35. Buttons
Primary Button:
Filled blue 
Rounded corners 
Soft shadow 
Hover elevation 
Secondary Button:
Transparent background 
Blue outline 
Blue text 
Hover:
Subtle color fill 
Smooth transition 
36. Glassmorphism Usage
Glass effects should be used sparingly.
Apply glass styling to:
Feature cards 
Call-to-action panels 
Floating information cards 
Do not apply glass effects to the entire page background.
37. Iconography
Use a single outline icon set (e.g., Lucide React).
Icons should represent:
Fleet 
Route 
Delivery 
Analytics 
Maintain consistent size and stroke width.
38. Responsive Design
Desktop
Two-column Hero layout.
Laptop
Maintain two-column layout with adjusted spacing.
Tablet
Hero becomes stacked:
Drone illustration moves below the text.
Navigation simplifies.
Mobile (basic support)
Single-column layout.
Buttons stack vertically.
Cards become full width.
39. Accessibility
Ensure:
Semantic HTML structure. 
Keyboard-accessible navigation. 
Sufficient color contrast. 
Descriptive alt text for illustrations. 
Focus indicators on interactive elements. 
40. React Component Hierarchy
LandingPage
│
├
──
 Navbar
│
├
──
 HeroSection
│   
├
──
 HeroText
│   
├
──
 HeroButtons
│   └── HeroIllustration
│
├
──
 FeaturesSection
│   
├
──
 FeatureCard 
×
4
│
├
──
 WhyChooseSection
│   
├
──
 InfoCard 
×
6
│
└── Footer
Each component should remain reusable and independently testable.
41. Acceptance Criteria
The Landing Page is considered complete when:
Navigation functions correctly. 
Hero section matches the approved design language. 
Feature cards display the four modules. 
"Get Started" navigates to the Login Page. 
Responsive behavior is verified. 
Visual consistency aligns with Volume 1. 
No broken layouts or accessibility issues exist. 
42. Locked Rules
The following decisions are locked:
Minimal, premium design. 
Five-section layout. 
Blue-accent visual identity. 
Drone illustration in the Hero section. 
Two primary CTA buttons. 
Four feature cards. 
Simple navigation bar. 
Glassmorphism used only on cards. 
Responsive behavior as specified. 
No redesigns should be made without updating the specification.
End of Volume 2 – Section 2
Estimated formatted length:
 
14–16 pages
DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 2 – UI/UX & FRONTEND SPECIFICATION
SECTION 3
Login Page Specification
43. Login Page Purpose
The Login Page acts as the secure gateway into the Drone Delivery Navigation System.
Unlike traditional authentication screens, this page should feel like the 
pilot authorization terminal
 for a futuristic drone command center.
The experience should communicate:
Security 
Precision 
Professionalism 
Premium software quality 
Confidence 
The Login Page is intentionally minimal, allowing users to authenticate quickly while reinforcing the application's visual identity.
44. Design Philosophy
The Login Page should appear as a 
natural continuation of the Landing Page
 and a preview of the Dashboard.
It must not look like a separate application.
The user should immediately recognize that they are entering the same system.
Design principles:
Minimal 
Elegant 
Spacious 
Calm 
Secure 
Glassmorphism 
Human-centered 
45. Page Layout
Unlike the Dashboard, 
the Login Page does not include the sidebar
.
Overall layout:
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                         Header Area                         |
|                                                             |
|                Floating Companion Drone                     |
|                                                             |
|                AI Robot (Visual 
Companion)   
               |
|-------------------------------------------------------------|
|                                                             |
|               Centered Glass Login Card                     |
|                                                             |
|-------------------------------------------------------------|
|              Soft Background / City Skyline                 |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
The login card is the visual focal point.
46. Layout Structure
The page is divided into three vertical zones.
Zone 1 — Hero Zone
Contains:
Companion Drone 
AI Robot (visual mascot only) 
Ambient lighting 
Purpose:
Introduce the Drone Command Center.
Zone 2 — Authentication Zone
Contains:
Login Card 
Email 
Password 
Login Button 
Social Login 
Secure Login Indicator 
This occupies the center of the page.
Zone 3 — Background
Contains:
Futuristic city skyline 
Soft gradient 
Ambient blur 
Decorative floating shapes 
The background should remain subtle.
47. Header Area
Unlike the dashboard header, this area is simplified.
Contains:
Project Logo 
Project Name 
Optional "Back to Home" link 
No dashboard widgets appear here.
48. Companion Drone
The companion drone is a 
decorative branding element
.
Purpose:
Reinforce project identity. 
Connect visually with the Dashboard. 
Prepare the user for the dashboard transition. 
Requirements:
Positioned above the login card. 
Slightly larger than other illustrations. 
Soft shadow. 
Floating appearance. 
Future Version:
Drone transition animation into the Dashboard.
Version 1.0:
Static illustration.
49. Companion Robot
The robot is a 
visual guide only
.
It does 
not
 provide AI functionality in Version 1.0.
Position:
To the right of the drone, looking toward the login card.
Purpose:
Create a friendly and humanized interface.
50. Authentication Card
The authentication card is the centerpiece of the page.
Dimensions:
Width: ~600–650px (desktop) 
Rounded corners (24–32px) 
Frosted glass effect 
Soft shadow 
Thin translucent border 
Padding:
Generous internal spacing.
The card should feel premium and uncluttered.
51. Card Content
The card contains:
Title 
Subtitle 
Email Input 
Password Input 
Remember Me Checkbox 
Forgot Password Link 
Login Button 
Divider 
Social Login Buttons 
Secure Login Indicator 
Layout should follow a clean vertical hierarchy.
52. Typography
Title
Pilot Authentication
Large, bold, centered.
Subtitle
Short sentence describing secure access to the Drone Command Center.
Example:
"Sign in to manage your drone fleet and delivery operations."
53. Input Fields

Email
Email validation 
Placeholder 
Icon 
Rounded corners 
Password

Validation 
Rounded corners

Remember Me
Checkbox.

(Feature placeholder for Version 1.0.)

Primary action.

Filled blue 
Rounded 
Full width 
Soft shadow 
States:
Default 
Hover 
Loading 
Disabled

Button shows spinner and disables additional clicks.

Display beneath the primary login.
Providers:
Google 
Microsoft 
GitHub 
Version 1.0:
Visual placeholders only unless authentication is implemented.
Buttons should match the application's design language.
57. Secure Login Indicator
Bottom section of the card.
Contains:
Lock icon 
Secure connection text 
Purpose:
Increase user confidence.
58. Validation Rules
Email:
Required 
Valid email format 
Password:
Required 
Minimum length validation 
Errors:
Displayed directly beneath the relevant field.
Use concise, user-friendly messages.
59. Authentication Flow
Landing Page
      │
      
▼
Login Page
      │
Enter Credentials
      │
      
▼
Validate Form
      │
      
▼
Send API Request
      │
      
▼
JWT Authentication
      │
      
▼
Success
      │
      
▼
Dashboard
Failed authentication keeps the user on the Login Page with an appropriate error message.
60. Dashboard Transition
Version 1.0:
Simple fade transition after successful login.
Future Version (2.0):
The companion drone animates upward and transitions into the dashboard drone, creating a seamless visual connection between the Login Page and the Dashboard.
The architecture should support this enhancement without requiring structural changes.
61. Background Design
The background should include:
Soft pastel gradient 
Futuristic skyline 
Ambient lighting 
Light geometric shapes 
Avoid heavy graphics that compete with the login card.
62. Responsive Design
Desktop
Centered login card.
Drone and robot remain above.
Laptop
Reduce spacing while maintaining proportions.
Tablet
Scale down the hero illustrations.
Login card remains centered.
Mobile
Single-column layout.
Drone illustration reduced.
Social login buttons stack vertically.

The Login Page is complete when:
Layout matches the approved design. 
Form validation works correctly. 
JWT authentication integrates successfully. 
Responsive behavior is verified. 
Loading and error states function as expected. 
Successful login redirects to the Dashboard. 
Visual identity aligns with the Landing Page and Dashboard. 
66. Locked Rules
The following decisions are 
locked
:
No sidebar on the Login Page. 
Companion drone positioned above the login card. 
Companion robot included as a visual element only. 
Glassmorphism login card centered on the page. 
"Pilot Authentication" as the primary heading. 
Blue-accent design language. 
Future drone transition reserved for Version 2.0. 
Simple fade transition in Version 1.0. 
No redesigns should be made without updating the specification.

DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 2 – UI/UX & FRONTEND SPECIFICATION
SECTION 4
Dashboard Specification
67. Dashboard Purpose
The Dashboard is the 
Central Drone Command Center
 of the application.
It acts as the operational hub from which users can access every major module of the system.
Unlike traditional dashboards filled with excessive widgets and statistics, this dashboard follows a 
minimal navigation-first philosophy
.
Its purpose is to:
Welcome the user. 
Provide quick access to the four core modules. 
Display essential system information. 
Maintain a premium, futuristic interface. 
Serve as the central navigation point of the application. 
The Dashboard should never overwhelm the user with unnecessary information.
68. Dashboard Design Philosophy
The Dashboard is inspired by premium enterprise software such as:
Apple Human Interface 
DJI Flight Controller 
Linear 
Stripe Dashboard 
Arc Browser 
Design principles:
Spacious 
Minimal 
Humanized 
Elegant 
Premium 
Easy to scan 
Navigation-first 
The Dashboard is intentionally simple because detailed operational data belongs inside individual modules.
69. Overall Layout
The Dashboard uses a 
fixed sidebar
 with a 
scrollable content area
.
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                               HEADER                                  |
+-------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
|                   |                                                   |
|                   |      Welcome Hero Section                         |
|                   |                                                   |
|    SIDEBAR        +---------------------------------------------------+
|                   |                                                   |
|                   |      Module Card Grid                             |
|                   |                                                   |
|                   |   
   (
Scrollable Content 
Area)   
                 |
|                   |                                                   |
+-------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
The sidebar and header remain fixed while only the main content scrolls.
70. Dashboard Structure
The Dashboard consists of four major regions.
Fixed Sidebar 
Fixed Header 
Welcome Hero Section 
Module Card Grid 
No additional operational widgets should appear on the Dashboard.
71. Fixed Sidebar (LOCKED)
The sidebar is the primary navigation element.
Dimensions
Width: 240–260px 
Full viewport height 
Fixed position 
Appearance
Frosted glass 
Soft blue tint 
Rounded right corners 
Thin border 
Subtle shadow 
Contents (Top to Bottom)
Application Logo 
Dashboard 
Fleet Management 
Route Optimization 
Delivery Management 
Analytics Dashboard 
Logout 
Behavior
Active item highlighted. 
Hover elevation. 
Smooth transitions. 
No layout shifts. 
Sidebar color never changes across modules. 
72. Header (LOCKED)
The header provides contextual information.
Layout
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
| Dashboard Title | Drone Illustration | Date | Time | Weather |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
Left Section
Page title 
Subtitle 
Center
Companion drone (locked visual element) 
Right
Date 
Time 
Weather 
Notifications 
User avatar 
The drone illustration must remain visually centered to create continuity with the Login Page.
73. Welcome Hero Section
The first content block welcomes the authenticated user.
Layout
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
| Welcome Message                                               |
| Brief description                                             |
| System Status Badge                                           |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
Purpose
Orient the user. 
Reinforce branding. 
Provide quick context. 
Suggested content:
Welcome back, Captain.
"Manage your drone fleet, optimize routes, monitor deliveries, and analyze performance from one intelligent command center."
74. Module Card Grid
This is the primary interactive region of the Dashboard.
Only 
four large portrait cards
 are visible initially.
The user scrolls to access additional content if introduced in future versions.
Grid Layout
+-----------+-----------+
| Fleet     | Route     |
| Management| Optimizer |
+-----------+-----------+
+-----------+-----------+
| 
Delivery  |
 Analytics |
| Management| Dashboard |
+-----------+-----------+
The cards should feel spacious and prominent.
75. Module Card Design
Each module card is a reusable component.
Dimensions
Portrait orientation 
Large clickable area 
Consistent height and width 
Structure
+-----------------------------------+
| Large Icon                        |
|                                   |
| Module Title                      |
|                                   |
+-----------------------------------+
Important:
 Only the icon and module title should be displayed. No descriptions or statistics should appear on the cards.
Interaction
Hover:
Slight elevation 
Soft glow 
Cursor pointer 
Click:
Navigate to the respective module. 
76. Module Identity
Each card uses a subtle accent color:
