23. User Experience Principles
The Route Optimization module should prioritize:
Interactive map-first experience. 
Minimal user effort. 
Immediate visual feedback. 
Clear operational information. 
Fast route generation. 
Consistent navigation. 
Smooth transitions. 
The interface should always make it obvious what the next action is.
24. Acceptance Criteria
The Route Optimization UI is complete when:
The layout matches this specification. 
The Dashboard sidebar and header are reused without modification. 
The Hero, KPI cards, Mapbox workspace, and control panel are implemented. 
Warehouse and destination selection work correctly. 
Route information updates after route generation. 
Comparison, loading, empty, and error states are implemented. 
Responsive behavior is verified across supported devices. 
25. Locked Specifications
The following decisions are 
non-negotiable
 for Version 1.0:
Emerald green accent theme. 
Dashboard sidebar reused without modification. 
Dashboard header reused without modification. 
Hero → KPI Cards → Map + Control Panel layout. 
Interactive Mapbox map as the primary workspace. 
Dedicated route control panel. 
Route comparison cards. 
No-fly zone status panel. 
Glassmorphism styling. 
Responsive split-screen layout. 
These specifications define the Route Optimization UI for Version 1.0 and must not be redesigned without updating the documentation.
End of Volume 4 – Section 1
DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 4 – ROUTE OPTIMIZATION MODULE SPECIFICATION
SECTION 2
Frontend Architecture
26. Purpose
The purpose of this section is to define the frontend architecture of the Route Optimization module.
The architecture should:
Be modular. 
Support interactive mapping. 
Be highly scalable. 
Separate UI from business logic. 
Be optimized for future real-time features. 
The module must integrate seamlessly with the global architecture defined in Volumes 1 and 2.
27. Frontend Architecture Philosophy
The Route Optimization module follows a 
feature-based architecture
.
Responsibilities are separated into:
UI Components 
Map Components 
Business Logic 
API Communication 
State Management 
Custom Hooks 
Utility Functions 
Type Definitions 
No component should have more than one primary responsibility.
28. Route Module Folder Structure
src/
modules/
route/
│
├
──
 pages/
│   └── RouteOptimizationPage.tsx
│
├
──
 components/
│   
├
──
 RouteHero/
│   
├
──
 RouteStatistics/
│   
├
──
 RouteWorkspace/
│   
├
──
 MapboxContainer/
│   
├
──
 WarehouseSelector/
│   
├
──
 DestinationSelector/
│   
├
──
 RouteInformation/
│   
├
──
 RouteComparison/
│   
├
──
 NoFlyZonePanel/
│   
├
──
 GenerateRouteButton/
│   
├
──
 SaveRouteButton/
│   
├
──
 RouteHistory/
│   
├
──
 EmptyState/
│   
├
──
 ErrorState/
│   └── LoadingSkeleton/
│
├
──
 hooks/
│   
├
──
 useMapbox.ts
│   
├
──
 useRoute.ts
│   
├
──
 useWarehouse.ts
│   
├
──
 useDestination.ts
│   └── useRouteComparison.ts
│
├
──
 services/
│   └── routeService.ts
│
├
──
 interfaces/
│   
├
──
 Warehouse.ts
│   
├
──
 Destination.ts
│   
├
──
 Route.ts
│   
├
──
 RouteStatistics.ts
│   └── RouteComparison.ts
│
├
──
 utils/
│   
├
──
 mapHelpers.ts
│   
├
──
 markerHelpers.ts
│   
├
──
 routeHelpers.ts
│   └── validators.ts
│
└── constants/
    
├
──
 markerColors.ts
    
├
──
 routeColors.ts
    └── mapSettings.ts
The Route module should remain completely isolated from other feature modules.
29. Page Hierarchy
RouteOptimizationPage
│
├
──
 MainLayout
│
├
──
 RouteHero
│
├
──
 RouteStatistics
│
├
──
 RouteWorkspace
│   
├
──
 MapboxContainer
│   └── RouteControlPanel
│
├
──
 RouteHistory
│
├
──
 LoadingState
├
──
 ErrorState
└── EmptyState
The page itself should only compose child components.
30. Component Responsibilities
Each component has a clearly defined responsibility.
RouteHero
Displays:
Page title 
Subtitle 
Generate Route button 
System status 
RouteStatistics
Displays:
Active Warehouses 
Available Destinations 
Routes Generated 
Efficiency Score 
Receives statistics via props.
MapboxContainer
Responsible only for:
Rendering the interactive Mapbox map. 
Displaying markers. 
Drawing routes. 
Rendering no-fly zones. 
Handling map events. 
No business logic should exist inside the map component.
WarehouseSelector
Responsible for selecting the route origin.
Displays warehouse information.
DestinationSelector
Allows users to search and select delivery destinations.
Supports future multi-stop routing.
RouteInformation
Displays:
Distance 
Time 
Battery usage 
Optimization score 
Waypoints 
RouteComparison
Displays multiple route options.
Highlights the recommended route.
NoFlyZonePanel
Displays:
Active restrictions 
Warning messages 
Route conflicts 
RouteHistory
Displays previously generated routes.
Allows future reloading of saved routes.
31. TypeScript Interfaces
Every data model should have a dedicated interface.
Warehouse
Fields:
id 
name 
latitude 
longitude 
status 
availableDrones 
Destination
Fields:
id 
name 
latitude 
longitude 
priority 
packageCount 
Route
Fields:
id 
warehouse 
destinations 
distance 
duration 
batteryUsage 
optimizationScore 
createdAt 
RouteStatistics
Fields:
activeWarehouses 
availableDestinations 
optimizedRoutesToday 
averageEfficiency 
RouteComparison
Fields:
routeName 
distance 
duration 
batteryUsage 
efficiencyScore 
Interfaces should be reused across components, hooks, and services.
32. State Management
The Route module primarily uses 
local state
.
Local State
Selected warehouse 
Selected destination(s) 
Generated route 
Loading state 
Error state 
Map zoom 
Selected comparison route 
Shared State
Only shared if needed across multiple modules:
User 
Authentication 
Notifications 
Route-specific state should remain inside the Route module.
33. Custom Hooks
Reusable logic should be extracted into hooks.
useMapbox(
)
Responsibilities:
Initialize Mapbox 
Load map 
Handle markers 
Draw route 
Clear route 
useRoute(
)
Responsibilities:
Generate route 
Save route 
Fetch route history 
Refresh route 
useWarehouse(
)
Responsibilities:
Load warehouses 
Select warehouse 
Retrieve warehouse information 
useDestination(
)
Responsibilities:
Search destinations 
Select destinations 
Validate selections 
useRouteComparison(
)
Responsibilities:
Load alternative routes 
Select preferred route 
Compare metrics 
Hooks should never render UI.
34. Service Layer
All backend communication passes through 
routeService.ts
.
Responsibilities:
Fetch warehouses 
Fetch destinations 
Generate optimized route 
Retrieve saved routes 
Save route 
Retrieve route statistics 
Compare routes 
Components must never communicate directly with Axios.
35. Mapbox Integration
The Route module uses 
Mapbox GL JS
.
Responsibilities:
Interactive map rendering 
Marker placement 
Route drawing 
Camera movement 
Map controls 
Zoom handling 
The map should be initialized only once during the page lifecycle.
36. Map Layers
The Mapbox implementation should include separate layers.
Layers:
Base Map 
Warehouse Markers 
Destination Markers 
Route Line 
No-Fly Zones 
Selected Route Highlight 
Keeping layers separate improves maintainability and rendering performance.
37. Marker Management
Marker types:
Warehouse
Destination
Selected Destination
Current Route
Future Drone Position (reserved)
Each marker should have:
Unique icon 
Popup 
Hover interaction 
38. API Flow
Every frontend interaction follows:
User Action
↓
React Component
↓
Custom Hook
↓
Route Service
↓
Axios
↓
Backend API
↓
Response
↓
Map Update
↓
UI Update
This sequence should remain consistent throughout the module.
39. Props Strategy
Components receive only the data they require.
Example:
RouteInformation receives:
distance 
duration 
batteryUsage 
optimizationScore 
It should not receive the full Route object unless required.
40. Event Flow
Example:
Generate Route
↓
GenerateRouteButton
↓
RouteOptimizationPage
↓
useRoute(
)
↓
routeService
↓
Backend
↓
Update Map
↓
Update Statistics
↓
Update Information Panel
UI components communicate upward through callbacks and receive updated state through props.
41. Validation Strategy
Client-side
Validate:
Warehouse selected 
Destination selected 
Valid coordinates 
Prevent submission if mandatory fields are missing.
Server-side
Validate:
Warehouse exists 
Destination exists 
Route request is valid 
Operational constraints 
Frontend validation improves usability but never replaces backend validation.
42. Loading Strategy
Independent loading states should exist for:
Initial page load 
Warehouse loading 
Destination loading 
Route generation 
Route saving 
Route comparison 
The map remains interactive whenever possible.
43. Error Handling
The module should handle:
Network failures 
Invalid selections 
Route generation failures 
Authentication failures 
Backend errors 
Error messages should be concise and user-friendly.
44. Performance Guidelines
To ensure smooth interaction:
Initialize Mapbox only once. 
Reuse markers instead of recreating them. 
Remove unused layers. 
Lazy-load route history. 
Minimize unnecessary re-renders. 
Memoize derived values where appropriate. 
The module should remain responsive even with larger datasets.
45. Accessibility
The Route Optimization module should support:
Keyboard navigation 
Screen reader compatibility 
Focus management 
Accessible forms 
High color contrast 
Map interactions should include accessible alternatives where feasible.
46. Testing Strategy
Component Tests
WarehouseSelector 
DestinationSelector 
RouteInformation 
RouteComparison 
Integration Tests
Generate route 
Save route 
Compare routes 
Load history 
Map Tests
Marker placement 
Route rendering 
Layer visibility 
Control interactions 
Testing should verify user-visible behavior.
47. Frontend Quality Checklist
Before completing the Route module, verify:
Components have a single responsibility. 
Mapbox is initialized correctly. 
Route generation updates the map. 
Route information refreshes after API responses. 
Custom hooks encapsulate logic. 
Service layer handles all API requests. 
Loading, error, and empty states exist. 
Responsive layout is implemented. 
Accessibility requirements are satisfied. 
48. Locked Frontend Rules
The following decisions are permanently locked for Route Optimization:
Feature-based folder structure. 
Dedicated Route module directory. 
React Functional Components only. 
TypeScript for all files. 
Tailwind CSS styling. 
Mapbox GL JS integration. 
Service-based API communication. 
Custom hooks for reusable logic. 
Interactive map as the central UI element. 
Separate Mapbox layers for markers, routes, and no-fly zones. 
Business logic isolated from presentation components. 
These rules define the frontend implementation for Version 1.0 and must not be changed without revising the specification.
End of Volume 4 – Section 2
DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 4 – ROUTE OPTIMIZATION MODULE SPECIFICATION
SECTION 3
Backend Architecture & Database Design
49. Purpose
The Route Optimization backend is responsible for processing route requests and transforming geographical information into optimized delivery paths.
Its responsibilities include:
Warehouse management 
Destination management 
Route generation 
Route storage 
Route comparison 
No-fly zone validation 
Authentication 
Database interaction 
Graph initialization 
API response generation 
The backend performs all optimization logic while the frontend focuses solely on visualization.
50. Backend Architecture Philosophy
The Route Optimization module follows the same 
Layered Architecture
 adopted across the project.
Every request follows this lifecycle:
Client
↓
Route API
↓
Authentication Middleware
↓
Validation Middleware
↓
Route Controller
↓
Route Service
↓
Graph Engine
↓
Algorithms
(Dijkstra / BFS)
↓
Repository
↓
MongoDB
↓
Response
Each layer performs a single, well-defined responsibility.
51. Route Module Folder Structure
server/
src/
modules/
route/
│
├
──
 routes/
│   └── 
route.routes
.ts
├
──
 controllers/
│   └── 
route.controller
.ts
├
──
 services/
│   └── 
route.service
.ts
├
──
 models/
│   
├
──
 
warehouse.model
.ts
│   
├
──
 
destination.model
.ts
│   
├
──
 
route.model
.ts
│   └── noFlyZone.model.ts
├
──
 algorithms/
│   
├
──
 graph.ts
│   
├
──
 dijkstra.ts
│   
├
──
 bfs.ts
│   └── graphBuilder.ts
├
──
 validators/
│   └── 
route.validator
.ts
├
──
 middleware/
│   └── 
route.middleware
.ts
├
──
 repository/
│   └── 
route.repository
.ts
└── interfaces/
    
├
──
 Warehouse.ts
    
├
──
 Destination.ts
    
├
──
 Route.ts
    └── Graph.ts
The Route module remains completely independent from Fleet, Delivery, and Analytics.
52. Layer Responsibilities
Routes
Responsibilities:
Define REST endpoints. 
Apply authentication middleware. 
Apply validation middleware. 
Forward requests to controllers. 
Routes should never contain business logic.
Controllers
Responsibilities:
Receive HTTP requests. 
Validate request context. 
Invoke services. 
Return standardized responses. 
Controllers remain thin.
Services
The Service Layer is the heart of the module.
Responsibilities:
Generate routes 
Compare routes 
Save routes 
Retrieve route history 
Build graph structures 
Invoke Dijkstra 
Invoke BFS 
Calculate route metrics 
All business logic belongs here.
Graph Engine
Responsible for:
Constructing weighted graphs 
Loading graph data 
Updating graph edges 
Removing blocked edges 
Passing graph structures to algorithms 
The Graph Engine isolates graph management from the rest of the backend.
Repository
Responsible only for database communication.
Responsibilities:
Read warehouses 
Read destinations 
Store routes 
Retrieve history 
Read no-fly zones 
No optimization logic belongs here.
53. MongoDB Collections
The Route module uses four primary collections.
warehouses
destinations
routes
noflyzones
Each collection has a dedicated schema.
54. Warehouse Schema
Fields:
_id
warehouseId
name
latitude
longitude
status
availableDrones
address
createdAt
updatedAt
Each warehouse represents a valid route origin.
55. Destination Schema
Fields:
_id
destinationId
name
latitude
longitude
priority
packageCount
address
createdAt
updatedAt
Destinations act as graph vertices.
56. Route Schema
Fields:
_id
routeId
warehouseId
destinationIds
distance
estimatedTime
batteryUsage
optimizationScore
algorithmUsed
createdBy
createdAt
Every generated route is stored for future analysis.
57. No-Fly Zone Schema
Fields:
_id
zoneName
polygonCoordinates
restrictionLevel
status
createdAt
Each document represents one restricted geographical region.
Future versions may synchronize this data dynamically.
58. Database Relationships
Logical relationships:
Warehouse
↓
Generates
↓
Route
↓
Contains
↓
Destination(s)
↓
Affected By
↓
No-Fly Zones
ObjectId references should be used wherever appropriate.
59. Database Indexes
Recommended indexes:
Warehouse
warehouseId 
Destination
destinationId 
priority 
Routes
routeId 
warehouseId 
createdAt 
NoFlyZone
status 
Indexes improve route retrieval and historical searches.
60. Authentication
Every Route endpoint requires JWT authentication.
Authentication Flow:
JWT
↓
Verify Token
↓
Authorized User
↓
Route APIs
Unauthorized users cannot generate or access routes.
61. Authorization
Supported roles:

62. Route REST APIs
Get Warehouses
GET
/api/routes/warehouses
Get Destinations
GET
/api/routes/destinations
Generate Route
POST
/api/routes/generate
Compare Routes
POST
/api/routes/compare
Save Route
POST
/api/routes/save
Route History
GET
/api/routes/history
Route Details
GET
/
api
/routes/:id
No-Fly Zones
GET
/api/routes/noflyzones
63. Standard API Response
Success
{
  "success": true,
  "message": "Route generated successfully.",
  "data": {}
}
Error
{
  "success": false,
  "message": "Unable to generate route.",
  "errors": {}
}
Every endpoint should follow this format.
64. Backend Processing Flow
Frontend
↓
Route Controller
↓
Route Service
↓
Graph Engine
↓
Graph Algorithms
↓
Repository
↓
MongoDB
↓
Repository
↓
Controller
↓
Frontend
The Graph Engine acts as an intermediary between business logic and algorithm execution.
65. Validation Rules
Before generating a route:
Validate:
Warehouse exists. 
Destination exists. 
Coordinates are valid. 
At least one destination selected. 
Route request is complete. 
Invalid requests should return structured validation errors.
66. Error Handling
The backend should handle:
Invalid warehouse 
Invalid destination 
Graph construction failure 
Route generation failure 
Authentication failure 
Authorization failure 
Database failure 
Unexpected exceptions 
Appropriate HTTP status codes should be returned.
67. Route Storage Strategy
Every successfully generated route should be stored with:
Warehouse 
Destination(s) 
Algorithm used 
Distance 
Time 
Battery estimate 
Generated timestamp 
Creator information 
This enables history, comparison, and analytics.
68. No-Fly Zone Processing
Before executing the optimization algorithm:
Load active no-fly zones. 
Determine affected graph edges. 
Exclude restricted edges from traversal. 
Pass the updated graph to the optimization engine. 
This ensures generated routes comply with operational restrictions.
69. Logging Strategy
Log significant backend events.
Examples:
Route generated 
Route saved 
Route comparison executed 
No-fly zone conflict detected 
Warehouse updated 
Destination updated 
Authentication failure 
Logs should aid debugging while avoiding sensitive data exposure.
70. Backend Security
Mandatory practices:
JWT authentication. 
Input validation. 
Sanitized request handling. 
Environment variable configuration. 
Role-based authorization. 
Protected route generation. 
Secure error responses. 
Internal implementation details must never be exposed.
71. Performance Guidelines
The backend should:
Cache static warehouse and destination data where appropriate. 
Build graphs efficiently. 
Avoid unnecessary database queries. 
Use indexes effectively. 
Minimize repeated graph construction. 
Keep route generation responsive. 
These practices support scalability as the dataset grows.
72. Backend Quality Checklist
Before completing the Route backend, verify:
REST APIs implemented. 
Authentication working. 
Authorization enforced. 
Validation middleware applied. 
Repository pattern followed. 
Graph Engine implemented. 
Database schemas complete. 
Indexes created. 
Standard response format used. 
Logging configured. 
73. Locked Backend Rules
The following decisions are permanently locked for Version 1.0:
Express.js backend. 
MongoDB with Mongoose. 
Layered architecture. 
Repository pattern. 
JWT authentication. 
Validation middleware. 
Dedicated Route module. 
Separate Graph Engine. 
RESTful APIs. 
Standard API responses. 
Independent graph algorithm layer. 
These specifications define the backend implementation for the Route Optimization module and should not be modified without updating the documentation.
End of Volume 4 – Section 3
DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 4 – ROUTE OPTIMIZATION MODULE SPECIFICATION
SECTION 4
Data Structures & Graph Algorithms Implementation
74. Purpose
The Route Optimization module is built around 
Graph Theory
, one of the most powerful applications of Data Structures and Algorithms.
Instead of storing locations independently, the system models the entire delivery network as a 
weighted graph
, allowing intelligent route computation.
This section explains:
Graph Representation 
Adjacency Lists 
Weighted Edges 
Dijkstra's Algorithm 
Breadth-First Search (BFS) 
No-Fly Zone Processing 
Route Comparison 
Time Complexity 
Space Complexity 
Backend Integration 
Frontend Visualization 
75. DSA Philosophy
The Route Optimization module should not generate routes randomly.
Every optimized route must be the result of a graph algorithm.
Workflow:
User Selects Warehouse
↓
User Selects Destination
↓
Graph Created
↓
No-Fly Zones Applied
↓
Graph Algorithm Executed
↓
Best Route Found
↓
Map Updated
The frontend never performs graph calculations.
76. Primary Data Structure — Graph
The Route Optimization module uses a 
Weighted Graph
.
Each warehouse and destination becomes a 
vertex
.
Road connections become 
weighted edges
.
Example:
Warehouse A -------- 5km -------- Point B
      |
      | 8km
      |
Destination C
The graph accurately represents the delivery network.
77. Graph Representation
Each node represents:
Warehouse 
Delivery Destination 
Waypoint (future expansion) 
Each edge represents:
Road 
Flight corridor 
Safe travel path 
Each edge stores:
Distance 
Estimated Time 
Battery Cost 
78. Adjacency List
Instead of an adjacency matrix, the project uses an 
Adjacency List
.
Example:
Warehouse
↓
Destination A (5)
↓
Destination B (8)
↓
Destination C (12)
Advantages:
Lower memory usage. 
Better scalability. 
Faster traversal for sparse graphs. 
Industry-standard representation. 
79. Graph Construction
Before route calculation:
Load Warehouses 
Load Destinations 
Load Road Connections 
Load No-Fly Zones 
Build Graph 
Workflow:
MongoDB
↓
Warehouse Data
↓
Destination Data
↓
Graph Builder
↓
Weighted Graph
The Graph Builder creates the in-memory graph.
80. Edge Weights
Every edge contains a weight.
Version 1.0 primarily uses:
Distance (km)
Future versions may incorporate:
Flight time 
Battery consumption 
Wind resistance 
Weather conditions 
Traffic restrictions 
Airspace congestion 
The architecture supports these future enhancements without modification.
81. Graph Builder
The Graph Builder is responsible for:
Creating vertices. 
Creating edges. 
Assigning weights. 
Building adjacency lists. 
Removing invalid edges. 
Passing the graph to optimization algorithms. 
It runs before any route generation request.
82. Dijkstra's Algorithm
Purpose
Find the 
shortest weighted path
 between the selected warehouse and destination.
This is the primary optimization algorithm used in Version 1.0.
Why Dijkstra?
Drone delivery requires minimizing travel distance while preserving operational efficiency.
Dijkstra guarantees the shortest path when edge weights are non-negative.
Workflow
Warehouse
↓
Priority Queue
↓
Nearest Vertex
↓
Relax Adjacent Edges
↓
Repeat
↓
Destination
↓
Shortest Route
Backend Flow
Generate Route
↓
Graph Builder
↓
Dijkstra
↓
Shortest Path
↓
Route Response
↓
Mapbox
The frontend simply renders the computed path.
83. Dijkstra Step-by-Step Example
Example graph:
           6
A ---------------- B
|                  |
2                  3
|                  |
C -------2---------D
 \
  \4
   \
    E
Shortest path from 
A
 to 
D
:
A → C → D 
Total Weight = 4 
Instead of:
A → B → D 
Total Weight = 9 
This demonstrates why Dijkstra produces optimal routes.
84. Dijkstra Time Complexity
Using a Priority Queue:
Time Complexity:
O(
(V + E) log V)
Where:
V = Vertices 
E = Edges 
This is efficient for large transportation networks.
85. Breadth-First Search (BFS)
Purpose
BFS is used for 
graph traversal and connectivity
, not weighted optimization.
Applications:
Verify destination reachability. 
Explore neighboring nodes. 
Detect disconnected regions. 
Validate graph connectivity. 
BFS Workflow
Warehouse
↓
Queue
↓
Visit Neighbor
↓
Visit Neighbor
↓
Visit Neighbor
↓
Destination Found
BFS ensures a valid path exists before optimization.
86. BFS Example
Warehouse
↓
A
↓
B
↓
C
↓
Destination
Traversal order:
Warehouse → A → B → C → Destination
This systematic exploration guarantees that every reachable node is visited.
87. BFS Time Complexity
Time Complexity:
O(
V + E)
Suitable for connectivity and reachability checks.
88. No-Fly Zone Processing
Before executing Dijkstra:
Load active no-fly zones. 
Identify graph edges intersecting restricted areas. 
Remove or disable those edges. 
Recalculate the graph. 
Workflow:
Original Graph
↓
Remove Restricted Edges
↓
Updated Graph
↓
Dijkstra
↓
Safe Route
This ensures generated routes comply with operational constraints.
89. Route Comparison Algorithm
The system may generate multiple candidate routes.
Comparison metrics:
Distance 
Estimated Time 
Battery Usage 
Number of Turns 
Efficiency Score 
The backend evaluates all candidates and recommends the best route.
90. Route Generation Lifecycle
Warehouse Selected
↓
Destination Selected
↓
Graph Loaded
↓
BFS Validation
↓
No-Fly Zones Applied
↓
Dijkstra Executed
↓
Shortest Route Found
↓
Database Updated
↓
Frontend Map Updated
Every generated route follows this sequence.
91. Backend Algorithm Integration
The Service Layer orchestrates graph processing.
Route Controller
↓
Route Service
↓
Graph Engine
↓
Graph Builder
↓
BFS
↓
Dijkstra
↓
Repository
↓
Frontend Response
Each algorithm remains isolated within the algorithms/ directory.
92. Frontend Interaction
The frontend never computes routes.
Instead:
Generate Route Button
↓
Route Service API
↓
Backend Graph Engine
↓
Optimized Route
↓
Mapbox Draws Path
↓
Route Information Updated
This separation ensures a single source of truth.
93. Time Complexity Summary

(V + E) log V)
<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="00F01E51" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:t>Shortest path
<w:trPr><w:tblCellSpacing w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/></w:trPr><w:tc><w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="05FA6EDD" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:t>Route Comparison
<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="737BD309" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:t>O(k)
<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="705BF20C" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:t>Compare candidate routes
Where 
k
 represents the number of candidate routes.
94. Space Complexity Summary
<w:tblPr><w:tblW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:tblCellSpacing w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/><w:tblCellMar><w:top w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/><w:left w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/><w:bottom w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/><w:right w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/></w:tblCellMar><w:tblLook w:val="04A0" w:firstRow="1" w:lastRow="0" w:firstColumn="1" w:lastColumn="0" w:noHBand="0" w:noVBand="1"/></w:tblPr><w:tblGrid><w:gridCol w:w="2068"/><w:gridCol w:w="1687"/></w:tblGrid><w:tr w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w14:paraId="62DD69C5" w14:textId="77777777"><w:trPr><w:tblHeader/><w:tblCellSpacing w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/></w:trPr><w:tc><w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="1A7784CB" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:pPr><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/></w:rPr><w:t>Algorithm

<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="35192BDD" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:proofErr w:type="gramStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:t>O(
V + E)
<w:trPr><w:tblCellSpacing w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/></w:trPr><w:tc><w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="24F75C40" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:lastRenderedPageBreak/><w:t>BFS
<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="409AD765" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:t>O(V)
<w:trPr><w:tblCellSpacing w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/></w:trPr><w:tc><w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="0EE7AF3F" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:t>Dijkstra
<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="693AB5A3" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:t>O(V)
<w:trPr><w:tblCellSpacing w:w="15" w:type="dxa"/></w:trPr><w:tc><w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="055DAA69" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:t>Priority Queue
<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="755F43C0" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:t>O(V)
The chosen representations balance memory efficiency with performance.
95. Why These Algorithms?
The selected algorithms solve distinct problems:
Graph
 models the delivery network. 
Adjacency List
 stores the network efficiently. 
BFS
 verifies connectivity. 
Dijkstra
 finds the optimal route. 
Priority Queue
 accelerates shortest-path computation. 
Route Comparison
 selects the most suitable path for presentation. 
Each algorithm contributes directly to user-visible functionality.
96. Future Algorithm Enhancements
The architecture reserves extension points for:
A* Search 
Bellman-Ford Algorithm 
Floyd-Warshall Algorithm 
Bidirectional Search 
Dynamic Programming for multi-stop optimization 
Real-time graph updates 
Weather-aware edge weighting 
Live obstacle avoidance 
These enhancements are outside the scope of Version 1.0.
97. DSA Quality Checklist
Before completing the Route module, verify:
Weighted Graph implemented. 
Adjacency List implemented. 
Graph Builder implemented. 
BFS implemented. 
Dijkstra implemented. 
Priority Queue used by Dijkstra. 
No-Fly Zone processing integrated. 
Route Comparison implemented. 
Algorithms isolated from controllers. 
Frontend consumes algorithm results only. 
98. Locked DSA Rules
The following decisions are permanently locked:
Weighted Graph as the primary data structure. 
Adjacency List for graph representation. 
Graph Builder responsible for graph construction. 
BFS for connectivity validation. 
Dijkstra for shortest-path optimization. 
Priority Queue supporting Dijkstra. 
No-Fly Zones processed before optimization. 
Algorithms isolated inside the algorithms/ directory. 
Backend exclusively performs graph computation. 
Frontend visualizes algorithm results through Mapbox. 
These specifications define the DSA implementation for the Route Optimization module and must not be modified without updating the documentation.
End of Volume 4 – Section 4

28–35 pages
DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 4 – ROUTE OPTIMIZATION MODULE SPECIFICATION
SECTION 5
Complete User Workflows, Business Logic & System Flow
99. Purpose
The purpose of this section is to define every user interaction within the Route Optimization module.
Each workflow documents:
User Action 
Frontend Behavior 
Backend Processing 
DSA Execution 
Database Operations 
API Communication 
UI Updates 
Every operation should follow a standardized lifecycle to ensure consistency and maintainability.
100. Route Optimization Workflow Philosophy
Every operation follows the same execution sequence.
User Action
↓
Frontend Validation
↓
Loading State
↓
API Request
↓
Authentication
↓
Backend Validation
↓
Graph Construction
↓
Graph Algorithms
↓
Database (Optional)
↓
API Response
↓
Map Update
↓
UI Update
↓
Success Notification
This workflow should remain consistent across all route-related features.
101. Workflow 1 — Open Route Optimization Module
User Goal
Access the route planning workspace.
User Action
Click 
Route Optimization
 from the dashboard sidebar.
Frontend
Load page layout. 
Display loading skeleton. 
Initialize Mapbox. 
Request warehouse data. 
Request destination data. 
Request statistics. 
Backend
Verify JWT. 
Fetch warehouses. 
Fetch destinations. 
Fetch route statistics. 
Database
Retrieve:
Warehouses 
Destinations 
Route statistics 
Result
Display:
Hero 
Statistics cards 
Interactive map 
Control panel 
102. Workflow 2 — Select Warehouse
User Goal
Choose the mission origin.
User Action
Select warehouse from dropdown.
Frontend
Highlight selected warehouse. 
Place marker on map. 
Update control panel. 
Backend
Retrieve warehouse details.
Result
Warehouse becomes active.
Future route calculations originate from this location.
103. Workflow 3 — Select Destination
User Goal
Choose delivery destination.
User Action
Search or select destination.
Frontend
Add destination marker. 
Display destination details. 
Enable Generate Route button. 
Backend
Validate destination.
Result
Warehouse and destination are ready for optimization.
104. Workflow 4 — Generate Route
User Goal
Generate the optimal delivery route.
User Action
Click 
Generate Route
.
Frontend
Disable button. 
Display loading indicator. 
Send request. 
API
POST
/api/routes/generate
Backend
Authenticate request. 
Validate selections. 
Build graph. 
Apply no-fly zones. 
Execute BFS. 
Execute Dijkstra. 
Calculate metrics. 
Store temporary result. 
Database
Read:
Warehouses 
Destinations 
No-fly zones 
Response
Return:
Optimized path 
Distance 
Time 
Battery estimate 
Efficiency score 
Frontend
Draw route on Mapbox. 
Update route information panel. 
Display success toast. 
105. Workflow 5 — Compare Routes
User Goal
Evaluate multiple route options.
User Action
Select 
Compare Routes
.
Frontend
Display comparison panel.
Backend
Generate alternative valid paths.
Compare:
Distance 
Time 
Battery usage 
Efficiency 
Result
Highlight recommended route.
Alternative routes remain available for inspection.
106. Workflow 6 — View Route Information
User Goal
Inspect optimization details.
User Action
Click generated route.
Frontend
Display:
Total distance 
Estimated time 
Battery usage 
Optimization score 
Waypoints 
Backend
Retrieve stored route metadata if needed.
Result
Users gain complete operational insight into the selected route.
107. Workflow 7 — Save Route
User Goal
Store optimized route.
User Action
Click 
Save Route
.
API
POST
/api/routes/save
Backend
Store:
Warehouse 
Destination(s) 
Route 
Metrics 
Timestamp 
User 
Database
Insert:
Route document.
Result
Display:
"Route saved successfully."
108. Workflow 8 — View Route History
User Goal
Access previously generated routes.
User Action
Open 
Route History
.
Frontend
Display saved routes.
Backend
Retrieve:
Historical routes.
Database
Query:
Routes collection.
Result
Display:
Route name 
Date 
Distance 
Time 
Efficiency 
Users can reload historical routes.
109. Workflow 9 — No-Fly Zone Detection
User Goal
Ensure generated route avoids restricted areas.
Backend
Before optimization:
Load active no-fly zones. 
Detect intersecting graph edges. 
Remove blocked edges. 
Recalculate graph. 
Frontend
Display:
🟢
 Safe Route
or
⚠
 Route Modified due to No-Fly Zone
Result
Only safe routes are displayed.
110. Workflow 10 — Regenerate Route
User Goal
Generate a new optimized route after changing selections.
User Action
Select another destination.
Click 
Generate Route
.
Backend
Repeat:
Graph construction 
BFS 
Dijkstra 
Route comparison 
Frontend
Remove previous path. 
Draw new optimized path. 
Update metrics. 
Result
Map always reflects the latest optimized route.
111. Business Rules
The Route Optimization module follows these mandatory rules.
A route:
Must have exactly one warehouse. 
Must have at least one destination. 
Cannot pass through active no-fly zones. 
Cannot be generated without valid coordinates. 
Must use Dijkstra for shortest-path optimization. 
Must verify connectivity using BFS before optimization. 
These rules are enforced on the backend.
112. Validation Rules
Before generating a route:
Validate:
✔
 Warehouse selected
✔
 Destination selected
✔
 Valid coordinates
✔
 Existing warehouse
✔
 Existing destination
✔
 Active graph
✔
 Reachable destination
Validation failures prevent unnecessary computation.
113. Notification Strategy
Successful operations display lightweight toast notifications.
Examples:
✅
 Warehouse Selected
✅
 Destination Added
✅
 Route Generated
✅
 Route Saved
⚠
 Route Updated
❌
 Route Generation Failed
Notifications automatically disappear after a few seconds.
114. Loading Strategy
Independent loading states:
Page initialization 
Warehouse loading 
Destination loading 
Route generation 
Route saving 
Route comparison 
The interactive map should remain visible whenever possible.
115. Error Handling
Possible failures include:
Invalid warehouse 
Invalid destination 
Authentication failure 
Graph creation failure 
No valid path found 
Database error 
Network error 
Server error 
Each error should provide a user-friendly explanation and, where appropriate, a retry option.
116. Performance Rules
The Route Optimization module should:
Cache static warehouse data when appropriate. 
Avoid rebuilding the graph unnecessarily. 
Reuse map layers. 
Fetch only required information. 
Update only affected UI sections. 
Maintain responsive interactions during route generation. 
These practices improve scalability and user experience.
117. Security Rules
All route-related operations require:
JWT authentication. 
Role-based authorization. 
Input validation. 
Protected APIs. 
Sanitized requests. 
Only authorized users may generate, save, or manage routes.
118. Accessibility Rules
The module should support:
Keyboard navigation. 
Screen readers. 
Focus management. 
Accessible forms. 
High contrast. 
Descriptive map alternatives where feasible. 
Interactive controls must remain accessible without relying solely on pointer devices.
119. Testing Checklist
Functional Tests
Select warehouse

Error state

120. Definition of Done
The Route Optimization module is complete when:
UI matches the approved specification. 
Frontend architecture follows Section 2. 
Backend architecture follows Section 3. 
Graph algorithms from Section 4 are fully integrated. 
Mapbox correctly visualizes generated routes. 
No-fly zones are respected. 
All workflows function correctly. 
Validation is enforced. 
Responsive behavior is verified. 
Accessibility requirements are met. 
Testing passes without critical issues. 
121. Locked Route Specifications
The following decisions are permanently locked for Version 1.0.
UI
Emerald green theme. 
Interactive Mapbox workspace. 
Hero section. 
Four KPI cards. 
Route control panel.

Graph Engine.

Adjacency List. 
Graph Builder. 
BFS. 
Dijkstra. 
Priority Queue integration. 
Backend-driven route computation. 
Business Rules
One warehouse per route. 
At least one destination. 
No traversal through active no-fly zones.

Dijkstra for shortest-path calculation. 
Backend remains the single source of truth for route computation. 
These specifications define the Route Optimization module for Version 1.0 and should not be modified without updating the documentation.

DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 5 – DELIVERY MANAGEMENT MODULE SPECIFICATION
SECTION 1
Delivery Management Overview & Complete UI Specification
Cover Statement
The Delivery Management Module is responsible for coordinating, monitoring, and controlling every delivery within the Drone Delivery Navigation System. It provides operators with a centralized workspace to create deliveries, assign drones, monitor delivery progress, manage priorities, and track the complete delivery lifecycle.
Unlike traditional logistics dashboards, this module is designed as a 
Delivery Operations Center
, where every delivery request moves through a structured workflow powered by efficient queue-based scheduling algorithms.
The module uses a 
minimal orange theme
, representing activity, urgency, and operational control while maintaining consistency with the overall application design.
1. Module Purpose
The Delivery Management Module enables users to:
Create new delivery requests. 
View all active deliveries. 
Assign optimized routes and drones. 
Monitor delivery status in real time. 
Track delivery progress. 
Prioritize urgent deliveries. 
Manage delivery queues. 
Cancel deliveries when required. 
View completed delivery history. 
Monitor operational workload. 
This module serves as the execution layer of the entire delivery system.
2. Module Objectives
Operational Objectives
Organize delivery requests. 
Reduce scheduling conflicts. 
Improve delivery efficiency. 
Prioritize urgent shipments. 
Track delivery progress. 
Simplify dispatch operations. 
Academic Objectives
Demonstrate practical implementation of:
Queue 
Priority Queue 
These data structures will drive delivery scheduling and assignment, with complete algorithmic details covered in Section 4.
3. Module Design Philosophy
The Delivery Management module should feel like entering a 
Delivery Dispatch Center
.
The interface should communicate:
Active operations 
Organization 
Delivery flow 
Priority handling 
Mission execution 
Operators should immediately understand:
Pending deliveries 
Active deliveries 
Completed deliveries 
High-priority requests 
4. Visual Identity
The Delivery Management module introduces a subtle orange identity.
Theme
Delivery Operations Center
Accent Color
Warm Orange
Background
White base 
Very light orange gradient 
Soft glassmorphism cards 
Rounded panels 
Minimal logistics-inspired abstract graphics 
The sidebar and header remain identical to every other module.
5. User Journey
Dashboard
      │
      
▼
Delivery Management
      │
      
├
──
 View Deliveries
      │
      
├
──
 Create Delivery
      │
      
├
──
 Assign Drone
      │
      
├
──
 Assign Route
      │
      
├
──
 Prioritize Delivery
      │
      
├
──

      │
      
├
──

      │
      └── View Delivery History
The workflow should remain intuitive and require minimal navigation.

 CreateDeliveryModal
├
──
 LoadingState
├
──
 EmptyState
└── ErrorState
Each component should have a single responsibility and be reusable.
24. User Experience Principles
The Delivery Management module should prioritize:
Clear delivery visibility. 
Fast assignment workflow. 
Easy status tracking. 
Minimal clicks. 
Immediate operational awareness. 
Smooth transitions between delivery stages. 
The interface should feel like a real dispatch center rather than a static management dashboard.
25. Acceptance Criteria
The Delivery Management UI is complete when:
The layout matches this specification. 
Dashboard sidebar and header remain unchanged. 
Hero, KPI cards, toolbar, delivery board, and timeline are implemented. 
Delivery cards display essential information. 
Details drawer and Create Delivery modal function correctly. 
Loading, empty, and error states are implemented. 
Responsive behavior works across supported screen sizes. 
26. Locked Specifications
The following decisions are 
non-negotiable
 for Version 1.0:
Minimal orange accent theme. 
Dashboard sidebar reused without modification. 
Dashboard header reused without modification. 
Hero → KPI Cards → Toolbar → Delivery Board layout. 
Four workflow columns: 
Pending 
Assigned 
In Transit 
Delivered 
Card-based delivery display. 
Right-side details drawer. 
Modal-based delivery creation. 
Glassmorphism styling. 
Responsive layout. 
These specifications define the Delivery Management UI for Version 1.0 and must not be redesigned without updating the documentation.
End of Volume 5 – Section 1
DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 5 – DELIVERY MANAGEMENT MODULE SPECIFICATION
SECTION 2
Frontend Architecture
27. Purpose
The purpose of this section is to define the frontend architecture for the Delivery Management module.
The architecture should:
Be modular. 
Support complex delivery workflows. 
Maintain separation of concerns. 
Be reusable. 
Support future real-time tracking. 
Integrate seamlessly with Fleet and Route modules. 
The frontend must remain lightweight while the backend performs business logic.
28. Frontend Architecture Philosophy
The Delivery Management module follows a 
Feature-Based Architecture
.
The module is divided into:
UI Components 
State Management 
Business Logic 
API Communication 
Custom Hooks 
Utility Functions 
Type Definitions 
Each file should have a single responsibility.
29. Delivery Module Folder Structure
src/
modules/
delivery/
│
├
──
 pages/
│   └── DeliveryManagementPage.tsx
│
├
──
 components/

──

──

The module should remain independent of other feature modules.
30. Page Hierarchy
DeliveryManagementPage
│
├
──
 MainLayout
│
├
──
 DeliveryHero
│
├
──
 DeliveryStatistics
│
├
──
 DeliveryToolbar
│

──
 DeliveryBoard
│

──
 PendingColumn
│

──
 AssignedColumn
│

──

│   └── DeliveredColumn

 DeliveryTimeline

──
 AssignDroneModal
│

──
 AssignRouteModal
│
├
──
 LoadingState
├
──
 ErrorState
└── EmptyState
The page acts only as the composition layer.
31. Component Responsibilities
Each component should perform one clearly defined task.
DeliveryHero
Displays:
Page title 
Subtitle 
Operations status 
New Delivery button 
DeliveryStatistics
Displays KPI cards.
Receives data through props.
Contains no API logic.
DeliveryToolbar
Handles:
Search 
Status Filter 
Priority Filter 
Refresh 
Create Delivery 
DeliveryBoard
Displays delivery workflow.
Contains all delivery columns.
DeliveryColumn
Displays deliveries belonging to one status.
Examples:
Pending 
Assigned 
In Transit 
Delivered 
DeliveryCard
Displays:
Delivery ID 
Customer 
Destination 
Priority 
Status 
Assigned Drone 
Contains presentation logic only.
DeliveryDrawer
Displays detailed delivery information.
Receives selected delivery as a prop.
DeliveryTimeline
Displays chronological delivery progress.
CreateDeliveryModal
Collects information for new deliveries.
Performs client-side validation.
AssignDroneModal
Displays available drones.
Allows operator selection.
AssignRouteModal
Displays available optimized routes.
Allows operator assignment.
32. TypeScript Interfaces
Every data model should have a dedicated interface.
Delivery
Suggested fields:
id 
deliveryId 
customerName 
warehouse 
destination 
assignedDrone 
assignedRoute 
priority 
status 
estimatedArrival 
packageWeight 
createdAt 
DeliveryStatistics
Contains:
pendingDeliveries 
activeDeliveries 
completedToday 
highPriorityDeliveries 
DeliveryPriority
Values:
High 
Medium 
Low 
DeliveryStatus
Values:
Pending 
Assigned 
In Transit 
Delivered 
Cancelled 
Interfaces should be reused throughout the module.
33. State Management
The Delivery module primarily relies on 
local state
.
Local State
Selected delivery 
Search query 
Status filter 
Priority filter 
Modal visibility 
Drawer visibility 
Current workflow column 
Shared State
Only if required globally:
Authentication 
User Profile 
Notifications 
Delivery-specific state should remain inside the module.
34. Custom Hooks
Reusable logic should be extracted into hooks.
useDelivery(
)
Responsibilities:
Fetch deliveries 
Refresh deliveries 
Create delivery 
Update delivery 
useDeliveryQueue(
)
Responsibilities:
Load pending queue 
Refresh queue 
Queue ordering 
usePriority(
)
Responsibilities:
Sort priorities 
Filter priorities 
Priority indicators 
useAssignment(
)
Responsibilities:
Assign drone 
Assign route 
Validate assignments 
useDeliveryHistory(
)
Responsibilities:
Fetch completed deliveries 
Filter history 
Retrieve delivery details 
Hooks should contain logic only and never render UI.
35. Service Layer
All backend communication passes through 
deliveryService.ts
.
Responsibilities:
Fetch deliveries 
Create delivery 
Update delivery 
Delete delivery 
Assign drone 
Assign route 
Retrieve statistics 
Fetch history 
Components must never communicate directly with Axios.
36. API Communication Flow
Every interaction follows:
User Action
↓
React Component
↓
Custom Hook
↓
Delivery Service
↓
Axios Client
↓
REST API
↓
Backend
↓
Response
↓
UI Update
This sequence should remain consistent throughout the module.
37. Props Strategy
Components should receive only the information they require.
Example:
DeliveryCard receives:
deliveryId 
customerName 
destination 
priority 
status 
assignedDrone 
Avoid passing complete objects unnecessarily.
38. Event Flow
Example:
Create Delivery
↓
CreateDeliveryModal
↓
DeliveryManagementPage
↓
useDelivery(
)
↓
deliveryService
↓
Backend
↓
Refresh Board
↓
Update Statistics
Events should propagate upward while state flows downward through props.
39. Validation Strategy
Client-side
Validate:
Customer Name 
Warehouse 
Destination 
Package Weight 
Priority 
Server-side
Validate:
Existing warehouse 
Existing destination 
Drone availability 
Route availability 
Business rules 
Frontend validation enhances usability but never replaces backend validation.
40. Loading Strategy
Independent loading states should exist for:
Page loading 
Delivery creation 
Delivery assignment 
Route assignment 
Board refresh 
History loading 
The interface should remain responsive during asynchronous operations.
41. Error Handling
Handle:
Validation failures 
Network failures 
Authentication failures 
Assignment failures 
Server errors 
Display concise, actionable error messages.
42. Performance Guidelines
The Delivery module should:
Render only visible deliveries. 
Use efficient filtering. 
Avoid unnecessary re-renders. 
Lazy-load delivery history. 
Refresh only affected workflow columns. 
Memoize expensive derived values when appropriate. 
These optimizations support larger delivery volumes.
43. Accessibility
Every interactive component should support:
Keyboard navigation 
Screen reader compatibility 
Focus management 
Accessible dialogs 
High color contrast 
Workflow columns should remain navigable without a mouse.
44. Testing Strategy
Component Tests
DeliveryCard 
DeliveryColumn 
DeliveryToolbar 
DeliveryTimeline 
Integration Tests
Create delivery 
Assign drone 
Assign route 
Status update 
View history 
User Flow Tests
Create → Assign → Transit → Delivered 
Testing should focus on observable behavior.
45. Frontend Quality Checklist
Before completing the Delivery frontend, verify:
Components follow single-responsibility. 
Feature-based folder structure implemented. 
TypeScript interfaces used consistently. 
Service layer handles API communication. 
Custom hooks encapsulate logic. 
Loading, error, and empty states implemented. 
Responsive layout verified. 
Accessibility requirements satisfied. 
46. Locked Frontend Rules
The following decisions are permanently locked for Version 1.0:
Feature-based folder structure. 
Dedicated Delivery module directory. 
React Functional Components only. 
TypeScript for all files. 
Tailwind CSS styling. 
Service-based API communication. 
Custom hooks for reusable logic. 
Delivery Board as the primary workspace. 
Presentation components free of business logic. 
Local state preferred over unnecessary global state. 
These rules define the frontend implementation for the Delivery Management module and must not be modified without revising the specification.
End of Volume 5 – Section 2
DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 5 – DELIVERY MANAGEMENT MODULE SPECIFICATION
SECTION 3
Backend Architecture & Database Design
47. Purpose
The Delivery Management backend is responsible for coordinating the complete lifecycle of every delivery.
Its responsibilities include:
Delivery CRUD operations 
Delivery assignment 
Delivery status management 
Queue management 
Priority handling 
Route assignment 
Drone assignment 
Authentication 
Database communication 
Business rule enforcement 
API response generation 
The backend acts as the execution engine of the logistics system.
48. Backend Architecture Philosophy
The Delivery module follows the same 
Layered Architecture
 used throughout the project.
Every request follows this lifecycle:
Client
↓
Delivery API
↓
Authentication Middleware
↓
Validation Middleware
↓
Delivery Controller
↓
Delivery Service
↓
Queue Engine
↓
Repository
↓
MongoDB
↓
Response
Each layer performs exactly one responsibility.
49. Delivery Module Folder Structure
server/
src/
modules/
delivery/
│
├
──
 routes/
│   └── 
delivery.routes
.ts
├
──
 controllers/
│   └── 
delivery.controller
.ts
├
──
 services/
│   └── 
delivery.service
.ts
├
──
 models/
│   
├
──
 
delivery.model
.ts
│   
├
──
 
package.model
.ts
│   └── deliveryHistory.model.ts
├
──
 algorithms/
│   
├
──
 queue.ts
│   
├
──
 priorityQueue.ts
│   └── assignmentEngine.ts
├
──
 validators/
│   └── 
delivery.validator
.ts
├
──
 middleware/
│   └── 
delivery.middleware
.ts
├
──
 repository/
│   └── 
delivery.repository
.ts
└── interfaces/
    
├
──
 Delivery.ts
    
├
──
 Package.ts
    
├
──
 DeliveryHistory.ts
    └── Assignment.ts
The Delivery module should remain completely independent from Fleet, Route, and Analytics modules.
50. Layer Responsibilities
Routes
Responsibilities:
Define REST endpoints. 
Apply authentication. 
Apply validation. 
Forward requests to controllers. 
No business logic.
Controllers
Responsibilities:
Receive requests. 
Validate request context. 
Invoke services. 
Return standardized responses. 
Controllers remain lightweight.
Services
The Service Layer is the core of the module.
Responsibilities:
Create deliveries. 
Assign drones. 
Assign routes. 
Update delivery status. 
Manage delivery queue. 
Execute Priority Queue. 
Retrieve delivery history. 
Generate statistics. 
All business rules belong here.
Queue Engine
Responsible for:
Managing delivery queue. 
Ordering pending deliveries. 
Executing Priority Queue. 
Determining assignment order. 
Preparing deliveries for dispatch. 
This layer isolates scheduling logic from business logic.
Repository
Responsible only for database operations.
Responsibilities:
Store deliveries. 
Retrieve deliveries. 
Update statuses. 
Store history. 
Fetch statistics. 
No scheduling or business logic should exist here.
51. MongoDB Collections
The Delivery module uses three primary collections.
deliveries
packages
deliveryHistory
Each collection has a dedicated schema.
52. Delivery Schema
Fields:
_id
deliveryId
customerName
customerContact
warehouseId
destinationId
assignedDrone
assignedRoute
priority
status
estimatedArrival
packageWeight
createdAt
updatedAt
Each document represents one delivery request.
53. Package Schema
Fields:
_id
packageId
deliveryId
weight
packageType
fragile
dimensions
createdAt
Allows future expansion for multiple packages per delivery.
54. Delivery History Schema
Fields:
_id
deliveryId
status
updatedBy
timestamp
remarks
Every status transition is stored for auditing and timeline generation.
55. Database Relationships
Logical relationships:
Delivery
↓
Assigned Drone
↓
Assigned Route
↓
Package
↓
Delivery History
Relationships should use ObjectId references wherever appropriate.
56. Database Indexes
Recommended indexes:
Deliveries
deliveryId (Unique) 
status 
priority 
assignedDrone 
Packages
deliveryId 
Delivery History
deliveryId 
timestamp 
Indexes improve queue processing and historical retrieval.
57. Authentication
Every Delivery endpoint requires JWT authentication.
Authentication Flow:
JWT
↓
Verify Token
↓
Authorized User
↓
Delivery APIs
Unauthorized users cannot modify deliveries.
58. Authorization
Supported roles:

59. Delivery REST APIs
Get Deliveries
GET
/api/deliveries
Create Delivery
POST
/api/deliveries
Get Delivery Details
GET
/
api
/deliveries/:id
Update Delivery
PUT
/
api
/deliveries/:id
Delete Delivery
DELETE
/
api
/deliveries/:id
Assign Drone
POST
/
api
/deliveries/:id/assign-drone
Assign Route
POST
/
api
/deliveries/:id/assign-route
Update Status
PATCH
/
api
/deliveries/:id/status
Delivery History
GET
/
api
/deliveries/:id/history
Delivery Statistics
GET
/api/deliveries/statistics
60. Standard API Response
Success
{
  "success": true,
  "message": "Delivery created successfully.",
  "data": {}
}
Error
{
  "success": false,
  "message": "Unable to create delivery.",
  "errors": {}
}
Every endpoint should return a consistent response format.
61. Backend Processing Flow
Frontend
↓
Delivery Controller
↓
Delivery Service
↓
Queue Engine
↓
Repository
↓
MongoDB
↓
Repository
↓
Controller
↓
Frontend
The Queue Engine handles scheduling before database updates occur.
62. Validation Rules
Before creating or updating a delivery:
Validate:
Customer name 
Warehouse 
Destination 
Package weight 
Priority 
Assigned drone availability 
Assigned route validity 
Invalid requests should return structured validation errors.
63. Assignment Strategy
Before assigning a drone:
Verify:
Drone exists. 
Drone is available. 
Route exists. 
Delivery is pending. 
Priority is valid. 
Only then should assignment proceed.
64. Status Lifecycle
Every delivery follows a fixed lifecycle.
Pending
↓
Assigned
↓
In Transit
↓
Delivered
Alternative paths:
Pending
↓
Cancelled
Status transitions must follow business rules.
65. Delivery History Management
Every status change generates a history record.
Examples:
Delivery created 
Drone assigned 
Route assigned 
Delivery dispatched 
Delivery completed 
Delivery cancelled 
History entries should never be deleted.
66. Logging Strategy
Log significant events.
Examples:
Delivery created 
Delivery assigned 
Route assigned 
Status updated 
Authentication failure 
Assignment failure 
Validation failure 
Logs should assist debugging without exposing sensitive information.
67. Backend Security
Mandatory practices:
JWT authentication. 
Role-based authorization. 
Input validation. 
Sanitized requests. 
Environment variables. 
Protected CRUD operations. 
Secure error responses. 
Internal implementation details must never be exposed.
68. Performance Guidelines
The backend should:
Process queue efficiently. 
Avoid unnecessary database queries. 
Cache static reference data when appropriate. 
Use indexes effectively. 
Update only affected delivery records. 
Keep assignment operations responsive. 
These optimizations support increasing delivery volumes.
69. Backend Quality Checklist
Before completing the Delivery backend, verify:
CRUD APIs implemented. 
JWT authentication applied. 
Authorization rules enforced. 
Validation middleware implemented. 
Queue Engine created. 
Repository pattern followed. 
Database schemas completed. 
Indexes created. 
Standard API responses used. 
Logging configured. 
70. Locked Backend Rules
The following decisions are permanently locked for Version 1.0:
Express.js backend. 
MongoDB with Mongoose. 
Layered architecture. 
Repository pattern. 
JWT authentication. 
Validation middleware. 
Dedicated Delivery module. 
Queue Engine abstraction. 
RESTful APIs. 
Standard API responses. 
Business logic isolated from controllers. 
These specifications define the backend implementation for the Delivery Management module and must not be modified without updating the documentation.
End of Volume 5 – Section 3
DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 5 – DELIVERY MANAGEMENT MODULE SPECIFICATION
SECTION 4
Data Structures & Algorithms (DSA) Implementation
71. Purpose
The Delivery Management module is responsible for scheduling, organizing, assigning, and tracking deliveries.
Instead of processing requests randomly, the system applies 
Queue
 and 
Priority Queue
 to manage delivery execution efficiently.
This section documents:
Queue 
Priority Queue 
Delivery Scheduling 
Assignment Logic 
Queue Operations 
Backend Integration 
Frontend Integration 
Time Complexity 
Space Complexity 
72. DSA Philosophy
Every delivery request should enter an organized scheduling system.
The system should process deliveries fairly while still supporting urgent requests.
General workflow:
Delivery Created
↓
Added to Queue
↓
Priority Evaluation
↓
Drone Assigned
↓
Route Assigned
↓
Delivery Executed
↓
Delivery Completed
The backend is solely responsible for executing scheduling algorithms.
73. Primary Data Structure — Queue
The Queue represents the list of delivery requests waiting to be processed.
It follows the 
FIFO (First In, First Out)
 principle.
Front
↓
Delivery A
↓
Delivery B
↓
Delivery C
↓
Rear
The first delivery created is normally processed first.
74. Queue Characteristics
Queue Operations:
Enqueue 
Dequeue 
Peek 
IsEmpty 
Size 
Applications in this module:
Pending deliveries 
Delivery scheduling 
Dispatch sequencing 
The Queue guarantees fair processing when priorities are equal.
75. Queue Workflow
Create Delivery
↓
Enqueue
↓
Pending Queue
↓
Assignment
↓
Dispatch
↓
Remove From Queue
Every delivery request enters the queue immediately after creation.
76. Enqueue Operation
Purpose:
Insert a new delivery request into the scheduling queue.
Workflow:
Create Delivery
↓
Validation
↓
Queue
↓
Database
↓
UI Updated
Time Complexity:
O(
1)
77. Dequeue Operation
Purpose:
Remove the next delivery for assignment.
Workflow:
Queue
↓
Front Delivery
↓
Assignment
↓
Status Updated
Time Complexity:
O(
1)
78. Queue Backend Integration
The Queue is maintained inside the Queue Engine.
Workflow:
Delivery Controller
↓
Delivery Service
↓
Queue Engine
↓
Queue
↓
Repository
↓
MongoDB
The frontend never manipulates queue structures directly.
79. Queue Frontend Integration
When a new delivery is created:
Create Delivery
↓
POST API
↓
Queue Engine
↓
Delivery Added
↓
Pending Column Updated
The Pending column reflects the current queue state.
80. Why Queue?
Queue is appropriate because:
Deliveries naturally arrive over time. 
Requests should normally be processed in arrival order. 
FIFO scheduling is easy to understand. 
Fairness is maintained. 
However, urgent deliveries require a different approach.
81. Priority Queue
A Priority Queue extends the basic queue by allowing higher-priority deliveries to be processed first.
Instead of:
A
↓
B
↓
C
The system becomes:
High
↓
Medium
↓
Low
Priority determines execution order.
82. Priority Levels
Supported priorities:
🔴
 High
🟠
 Medium
🟢
 Low
High-priority deliveries always move ahead of lower-priority requests.
83. Priority Queue Workflow
Delivery Created
↓
Priority Assigned
↓
Priority Queue
↓
Highest Priority Selected
↓
Assignment
↓
Dispatch
The highest-priority delivery is always selected first.
84. Priority Factors
Delivery priority may depend on:
User-selected urgency 
Medical supplies 
Express shipment 
Customer SLA 
Operational requirements 
Version 1.0 primarily uses manually selected priority levels.
The architecture supports automatic prioritization in future versions.
85. Assignment Engine
The Assignment Engine retrieves the next delivery from the Priority Queue.
Workflow:
Priority Queue
↓
Highest Priority Delivery
↓
Available Drone
↓
Optimized Route
↓
Assignment
↓
Status Updated
The Assignment Engine coordinates Fleet and Route modules.
86. Delivery Scheduling Algorithm
Complete scheduling process:
Delivery Request
↓
Validation
↓
Priority Queue
↓
Highest Priority
↓
Drone Availability Check
↓
Route Availability Check
↓
Assignment
↓
Dispatch
Only valid deliveries proceed to dispatch.
87. Queue Operations Summary

The implementation should use a binary heap internally.
89. Backend Algorithm Integration
The Service Layer invokes queue operations.
Delivery Controller
↓
Delivery Service
↓
Queue Engine
↓
Priority Queue
↓
Repository
↓
MongoDB
Algorithms remain isolated from controllers and repositories.
90. Frontend Interaction
The frontend requests scheduling operations rather than performing them.
Examples:
Create Delivery
Create Delivery
↓
POST API
↓
Queue Engine
↓
Pending Board Updated
Assign Delivery
Assign Delivery
↓
POST API
↓
Priority Queue
↓
Drone Assigned
↓
Board Updated
Update Status
Status Button
↓
PATCH API
↓
Queue Updated
↓
Timeline Updated
The frontend only visualizes algorithm results.
91. Delivery Lifecycle Algorithm
Created
↓
Queued
↓
Prioritized
↓
Assigned
↓
In Transit
↓
Delivered
↓
Archived
Every delivery follows this lifecycle.

93. Space Complexity Summary

<w:tcPr><w:tcW w:w="0" w:type="auto"/><w:vAlign w:val="center"/><w:hideMark/></w:tcPr><w:p w14:paraId="76BB18BE" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="006670E2" w:rsidRPr="006670E2" w:rsidRDefault="006670E2" w:rsidP="006670E2"><w:r w:rsidRPr="006670E2"><w:t>O(n)
Where 
n
 is the number of pending delivery requests.
94. Why These Data Structures?
Each structure solves a specific operational problem.
Queue
 maintains fair delivery ordering. 
Priority Queue
 handles urgent deliveries. 
Assignment Engine
 coordinates scheduling with Fleet and Route modules. 
Together they provide a practical demonstration of DSA concepts in logistics.
95. Future Algorithm Enhancements
The architecture supports future additions without restructuring.
Possible enhancements:
Circular Queue 
Double-Ended Queue (Deque) 
Multi-Level Priority Queue 
Weighted Priority Scheduling 
Deadline-Based Scheduling 
Dynamic Priority Adjustment 
AI-assisted dispatch (future version) 
These are beyond the scope of Version 1.0.
96. DSA Quality Checklist
Before completing the Delivery module, verify:
Queue implemented. 
FIFO scheduling supported. 
Priority Queue implemented. 
Binary Heap used internally. 
Assignment Engine integrated. 
Queue Engine isolated. 
Backend controls scheduling. 
Frontend visualizes queue state. 
Time complexity documented. 
Space complexity documented. 
97. Locked DSA Rules
The following decisions are permanently locked:
Queue used for standard delivery scheduling. 
Priority Queue used for urgent deliveries. 
Binary Heap implementation for Priority Queue. 
Queue Engine responsible for scheduling. 
Assignment Engine responsible for dispatch. 
Algorithms isolated inside the algorithms/ directory. 
Controllers never manipulate queue structures. 
Service Layer orchestrates scheduling. 
Backend remains the single source of truth. 
Frontend only visualizes scheduling results. 
These specifications define the DSA implementation for the Delivery Management module and should not be modified without updating the documentation.
End of Volume 5 – Section 4
DRONE DELIVERY NAVIGATION SYSTEM
VOLUME 5 – DELIVERY MANAGEMENT MODULE SPECIFICATION
SECTION 5
Complete User Workflows, Business Logic & Locked Specifications
98. Purpose
The purpose of this section is to define every user workflow within the Delivery Management module.
Each workflow includes:
User Action 
Frontend Behavior 
Backend Processing 
Queue Processing 
Database Operations 
API Communication 
UI Updates 
Every operation follows a standardized lifecycle to ensure consistency and maintainability.
99. Delivery Workflow Philosophy
Every delivery operation follows the same execution sequence.
User Action
↓
Frontend Validation
↓
Loading State
↓
API Request
↓
Authentication
↓
Backend Validation
↓
Queue Engine
↓
Business Rules
↓
Database Operation
↓
API Response
↓
UI Update
↓
Success Notification
This workflow remains consistent across all delivery-related operations.
100. Workflow 1 — Open Delivery Management Module
User Goal
Access the Delivery Operations Center.
User Action
Click 
Delivery Management
 from the dashboard sidebar.
Frontend
Load page layout. 
Display loading skeleton. 
Request delivery statistics. 
Request active deliveries. 
Request pending deliveries. 
Backend
Verify JWT. 
Retrieve deliveries. 
Retrieve statistics. 
Build workflow board. 
Database
Retrieve:
Deliveries 
Statistics 
Recent activity 
Result
Display:
Hero 
KPI Cards 
Delivery Toolbar 
Delivery Board 
Timeline 
101. Workflow 2 — Create Delivery
User Goal
Create a new delivery request.
User Action
Click:
+ New Delivery
Frontend
Open modal.
Validate:
Customer Name 
Warehouse 
Destination 
Package Weight 
Priority 
API
POST
/api/deliveries
Backend
Authenticate. 
Validate request. 
Create delivery. 
Add delivery to Queue. 
Save to database. 
Database
Insert:
New Delivery.
Result
Delivery appears in 
Pending
 column. 
Statistics refresh. 
Success notification displayed. 
102. Workflow 3 — Assign Drone
User Goal
Assign an available drone to a pending delivery.
User Action
Click:
Assign Drone
Frontend
Open drone selection modal.
Display available drones.
API
POST
/
api
/deliveries/:id/assign-drone
Backend
Validate delivery. 
Retrieve available drones. 
Verify drone availability. 
Assign drone. 
Database
Update:
assignedDrone
Result
Delivery card updates immediately.
103. Workflow 4 — Assign Route
User Goal
Attach an optimized route to the delivery.
User Action
Click:
Assign Route
Frontend
Display available optimized routes.
Backend
Retrieve:
Saved optimized routes.
Validate compatibility.
Assign selected route.
Database
Update:
assignedRoute
Result
Delivery now contains both drone and route assignments.
104. Workflow 5 — Priority Scheduling
User Goal
Ensure urgent deliveries are dispatched first.
Backend
Delivery enters:
Priority Queue.
Priority Queue evaluates:
High 
Medium 
Low 
Highest priority delivery is selected.
Result
Queue order updates automatically.
105. Workflow 6 — Dispatch Delivery
User Goal
Begin delivery execution.
User Action
Click:
Dispatch
Backend
Verify:
Drone assigned. 
Route assigned. 
Delivery pending. 
Update:
Status → Assigned
Immediately afterwards:
Status → In Transit
Database
Update delivery record.
Store history entry.
Result
Delivery moves to the 
In Transit
 column.
106. Workflow 7 — Track Delivery Progress
User Goal
Monitor delivery progress.
Frontend
Timeline updates.
Delivery status updates.
Progress indicator updates.
Backend
Retrieve latest delivery status.
Database
Read:
Delivery history.
Result
Timeline displays:
Created
↓
Queued
↓
Assigned
↓
In Transit
↓
Delivered
107. Workflow 8 — Complete Delivery
User Goal
Mark delivery as completed.
User Action
Click:
Complete Delivery
API
PATCH
/
api
/deliveries/:id/status
Backend
Update:
Status
↓
Delivered
Archive history.
Database
Update delivery.
Insert history record.
Result
Delivery moves to 
Delivered
 column.
Statistics refresh automatically.
108. Workflow 9 — View Delivery Details
User Goal
Inspect complete delivery information.
User Action
Click:
View Details
Frontend
Open right-side drawer.
Backend
Retrieve delivery.
Retrieve history.
Result
Display:
Customer 
Warehouse 
Destination 
Drone 
Route 
Timeline 
Package 
Priority 
Status 
109. Workflow 10 — Search & Filter Deliveries
User Goal
Quickly locate deliveries.
User Action
Use:
Search Bar
or
Filters
Backend
Retrieve filtered deliveries.
Frontend
Update board.
No page refresh required.
110. Business Rules
Every delivery must satisfy these rules.
A delivery:
Must belong to one warehouse. 
Must have one destination. 
Must have a valid priority. 
Cannot be dispatched without an assigned drone. 
Cannot be dispatched without an assigned route. 
Cannot move backward in the workflow. 
Must maintain a complete history. 
These rules are enforced on the backend.
111. Validation Rules
Before creating or updating a delivery:
Validate:
✔
 Customer Name
✔
 Warehouse
✔
 Destination
✔
 Package Weight
✔
 Priority
✔
 Drone Availability
✔
 Route Availability
✔
 Status Transition
Invalid requests return structured validation errors.
112. Notification Strategy
Successful operations display toast notifications.
Examples:
✅
 Delivery Created
🚁
 Drone Assigned
🗺
 Route Assigned
📦
 Delivery Dispatched
✔
 Delivery Completed
⚠
 Validation Failed
Notifications automatically disappear after a few seconds.
113. Loading Strategy
Independent loading states:
Page loading 
Delivery creation 
Drone assignment 
Route assignment 
Status update 
History loading 
Only affected components display loading indicators.
114. Error Handling
Possible failures:
Invalid delivery. 
Drone unavailable. 
Route unavailable. 
Authentication failure. 
Validation failure. 
Server error. 
Database error. 
Network error. 
Errors should provide meaningful guidance to the user.
115. Performance Rules
The Delivery Management module should:
Refresh only affected workflow columns. 
Avoid unnecessary API requests. 
Lazy-load history. 
Cache static reference data where appropriate. 
Maintain responsive interactions with large delivery volumes. 
116. Security Rules
All delivery operations require:
JWT authentication. 
Role-based authorization. 
Input validation. 
Protected REST APIs. 
Sanitized requests. 
Sensitive operations should only be accessible to authorized users.
117. Accessibility Rules
The module should support:
Keyboard navigation. 
Screen reader compatibility. 
Focus management. 
Accessible dialogs. 
High color contrast. 
The workflow board should remain usable without relying solely on mouse interactions.
118. Testing Checklist
Functional Tests
Create delivery. 
Assign drone. 
Assign route. 
Dispatch delivery. 
Update status. 
Complete delivery.

Filter. 
UI Tests
Responsive layout.

Error state. 
Backend Tests
CRUD APIs.

Authorization. 
Validation. 
Queue Engine. 
DSA Tests
Queue operations. 
Priority Queue operations. 
Assignment Engine. 
FIFO behavior. 
Priority scheduling. 
119. Definition of Done
The Delivery Management module is complete when:
UI matches the approved specification. 
Frontend follows Section 2. 
Backend follows Section 3. 
Queue and Priority Queue from Section 4 are fully integrated. 
Delivery lifecycle works correctly. 
Validation rules are enforced. 
Responsive behavior is verified. 
Accessibility requirements are met. 
Testing passes successfully. 
No critical defects remain.

The following decisions are permanently locked for Version 1.0.
UI

Dashboard header reused without modification. 
Hero section.

Validation middleware. 
DSA
Queue. 
Priority Queue.

Backend scheduling. 
FIFO processing. 
Binary Heap implementation for Priority Queue.

One warehouse per delivery. 
One destination per delivery. 
Drone required before dispatch.

Backend remains the single source of truth. 
These specifications define the Delivery Management module for Version 1.0 and must not be modified without updating the documentation.
